front: In the introductory finite setup of Chapter 1, $X$ is a finite set of observable events and $\Theta$ a finite set of explanations, following a joint distribution with $P(X)>0$. State Bayes's formula for $P(\theta\mid X)$, explain the role of each factor (prior, likelihood, normalizing constant), and give the slogan it leads to.
back:
For a finite set of observations $X$ and explanations $\theta$ following a joint distribution with $P(X)>0$, the usual formula for conditional probabilities gives
$$P(\theta\mid X)=\frac{P(\theta\text{ and }X)}{P(X)}=\frac{P(X\mid\theta)\,P(\theta)}{P(X)}.$$
This is Bayes's formula, which underlies all of Bayesian statistics. Here:
- $P(\theta)$ is the prior probability assigned to explanation $\theta$ (its plausibility before seeing data);
- $P(X\mid\theta)$ is the likelihood function;
- $P(X)=\sum_{\theta}P(X\mid\theta)P(\theta)$ is the normalizing constant ensuring $P(\cdot\mid X)$ is a probability distribution.
In words, $\text{posterior}\propto\text{likelihood}\times\text{prior}$. When $X$ and/or $\theta$ are continuum-valued (e.g. in $\mathbb{R}$ or $\mathbb{R}^d$), all probabilities are replaced by densities and $\{X=x\}$ is a probability-zero event, so the same formula must be justified rigorously.

points.label: Identifies prior, likelihood, normalizing constant correctly
States slogan posterior $\propto$ likelihood $\times$ prior (and notes density/justification issue in the continuous case)

front: Describe the Chapter 1 classification example: predictors $X\in\mathcal{X}$ (finite, $P(X)>0$) and label $\theta\in\{0,1\}$ with joint law $P$. Define the error probability of a classifier, derive the optimal classifier minimizing it, and name the resulting rule and associated loss.
back:
**Setup.** Let $\mathcal{X}$ be finite, let pairs $(X,\theta)\in\mathcal{X}\times\{0,1\}$ have joint law $P$, and assume $P(X=x)>0$ for every $x\in\mathcal{X}$ (so all conditional probabilities below are well defined). A **classifier** is any function $\hat\theta:\mathcal{X}\to\{0,1\}$. Its **error probability** is
$$R(\hat\theta)=P\big(\hat\theta(X)\neq\theta\big).$$
We seek $\hat\theta$ minimizing $R$.

**Step 1 â€” Conditioning on $X$.** By the tower property,
$$R(\hat\theta)=\mathbb{E}\big[\,P(\hat\theta(X)\neq\theta\mid X)\,\big].$$
Fix $x\in\mathcal{X}$. The classifier is deterministic, so given $X=x$ the prediction $\hat\theta(x)\in\{0,1\}$ is a fixed number, and the event $\{\hat\theta(X)\neq\theta\}$ is exactly $\{\theta\neq\hat\theta(x)\}$. Hence the **conditional error at $x$** is
$$r_x(\hat\theta):=P(\hat\theta(X)\neq\theta\mid X=x)=P\big(\theta\neq\hat\theta(x)\mid X=x\big).$$
Writing this out with indicators of the two possible predictions,
$$r_x(\hat\theta)=\mathbf{1}_{\{\hat\theta(x)=0\}}\,P(\theta=1\mid X=x)+\mathbf{1}_{\{\hat\theta(x)=1\}}\,P(\theta=0\mid X=x),$$
and therefore
$$R(\hat\theta)=\mathbb{E}\Big[\,\mathbf{1}_{\{\hat\theta(X)=0\}}\,P(\theta=1\mid X)+\mathbf{1}_{\{\hat\theta(X)=1\}}\,P(\theta=0\mid X)\,\Big].$$

**Step 2 â€” The two indicators are mutually exclusive.** For each $x$, exactly one of $\hat\theta(x)=0$, $\hat\theta(x)=1$ holds, so exactly one indicator above equals $1$ and the other equals $0$. Consequently $r_x(\hat\theta)$ is a **two-valued** quantity:
$$r_x(\hat\theta)=\begin{cases}P(\theta=1\mid X=x), & \text{if }\hat\theta(x)=0,\\[2pt] P(\theta=0\mid X=x), & \text{if }\hat\theta(x)=1.\end{cases}$$
In words: predicting $0$ incurs the conditional error $P(\theta=1\mid X=x)$ (the chance the truth was $1$), and predicting $1$ incurs $P(\theta=0\mid X=x)$.

**Step 3 â€” Pointwise minimization.** Because $\hat\theta(x)$ can be chosen freely and independently for each $x$, and because $r_x(\hat\theta)$ depends on $\hat\theta$ only through the single value $\hat\theta(x)$, minimizing $r_x$ over the two options gives
$$\min_{a\in\{0,1\}} r_x = \min\big\{P(\theta=1\mid X=x),\,P(\theta=0\mid X=x)\big\},$$
attained by predicting the class with the **smaller** opposing error. Now use that the two posteriors at $x$ sum to one:
$$P(\theta=0\mid X=x)+P(\theta=1\mid X=x)=1.$$
Since $t\mapsto 1-t$ is decreasing, the smaller of the two errors corresponds to the **larger** posterior: choosing $\hat\theta(x)=1$ (error $P(\theta=0\mid x)$) is best precisely when $P(\theta=1\mid x)\ge P(\theta=0\mid x)$, and symmetrically for $0$. Hence the pointwise minimizer is
$$\hat\theta(x)=\arg\max_{k\in\{0,1\}} P(\theta=k\mid X=x),$$
with the convention of breaking ties arbitrarily (a tie means $P(\theta=0\mid x)=P(\theta=1\mid x)=\tfrac12$, so either choice attains the same value $\tfrac12$).

**Step 4 â€” Pointwise optimal $\Rightarrow$ globally optimal.** The map $\hat\theta\mapsto r_x(\hat\theta)$ is a per-$x$ nonnegative quantity, and the classifier's values at distinct points $x$ are unconstrained relative to one another. Thus for **any** classifier $\hat\theta$ and the rule $\hat\theta^\star$ defined in Step 3,
$$r_x(\hat\theta)\ \ge\ r_x(\hat\theta^\star)=\min_k P(\theta\neq k\mid X=x)\qquad\text{for every }x\in\mathcal{X}.$$
Taking expectation over $X$ preserves this inequality (the integrand is nonnegative and dominated; $\mathcal{X}$ is finite so the expectation is a finite weighted sum with weights $P(X=x)>0$):
$$R(\hat\theta)=\mathbb{E}[\,r_X(\hat\theta)\,]\ \ge\ \mathbb{E}[\,r_X(\hat\theta^\star)\,]=R(\hat\theta^\star).$$
Since $\hat\theta$ was arbitrary, $\hat\theta^\star$ minimizes the error probability over all classifiers. (Minimizing the integrand pointwise minimizes the integral precisely because we are free to optimize each $x$ separately and no term can be made smaller than its own minimum.)

**Step 5 â€” Bayes' formula for the posterior.** The optimal rule is computed via Bayes' formula:
$$P(\theta=k\mid X=x)=\frac{P(X=x\mid\theta=k)\,P(\theta=k)}{P(X=x)},\qquad k\in\{0,1\},$$
i.e. posterior $\propto$ likelihood $\times$ prior. The optimal classifier
$$\hat\theta^\star(x)=\arg\max_{k\in\{0,1\}} P(\theta=k\mid X=x)$$
is the **Bayes classifier**.

**Step 6 â€” Identification of the loss and the minimal value.** With the $0$â€“$1$ loss $L(a,\theta)=\mathbf{1}_{\{a\neq\theta\}}$, the error probability is the expected loss
$$R(\hat\theta)=\mathbb{E}\big[L(\hat\theta(X),\theta)\big]=P(\hat\theta(X)\neq\theta),$$
so $R$ is exactly the risk under $0$â€“$1$ loss, and the Bayes classifier is its minimizer. The attained minimal (Bayes) error is
$$R(\hat\theta^\star)=\mathbb{E}\big[\min\{P(\theta=0\mid X),\,P(\theta=1\mid X)\}\big].$$

points.label: Defines error probability $R(\hat\theta)=P(\hat\theta(X)\neq\theta)$ and its conditional decomposition
Derives optimal rule $=\arg\max_k P(\theta=k\mid X=x)$ (argmax posterior)
Names Bayes classifier and links to $0$â€“$1$ loss / Bayes risk

front: Present the Chapter 1 Kalman-filter example as recursive Bayesian inference in a linear-Gaussian model: state the state-evolution (prior) and observation (likelihood) equations, and give the prediction/update recursion for the posterior of $\theta_t$ given $X_{1:t}$.
back:
A latent state $\theta_t\in\mathbb{R}^d$ evolves over time and is observed noisily via $X_t\in\mathbb{R}^m$:
- State evolution (provides the prior): $\theta_t=\Phi_t\theta_{t-1}+w_t$, $w_t\sim N(0,Q_t)$, so $\theta_t\mid\theta_{t-1}\sim N(\Phi_t\theta_{t-1},Q_t)$.
- Observation model (provides the likelihood): $X_t=A_t\theta_t+v_t$, $v_t\sim N(0,R_t)$, so $X_t\mid\theta_t\sim N(A_t\theta_t,R_t)$.
Because both prior and likelihood are Gaussian, the posterior is Gaussian (conjugacy), so it suffices to track mean and covariance. Given $\theta_{t-1}\mid X_{1:t-1}\sim N(\mu_{t-1},\Sigma_{t-1})$:
- Prediction: prior $\Pi_t=N(\mu_t^-,\Sigma_t^-)$ with $\mu_t^-=\Phi_t\mu_{t-1}$, $\Sigma_t^-=\Phi_t\Sigma_{t-1}\Phi_t^\top+Q_t$.
- Update: with residual $r_t=X_t-A_t\mu_t^-$ (so $\log\ell(X_t\mid\theta_t)=-\tfrac12 r_t^\top R_t^{-1}r_t+\text{const}$), the posterior is $\Pi_t(\cdot\mid X_t)=N(\mu_t,\Sigma_t)$ with $\mu_t=\mu_t^-+K_t r_t$, $\Sigma_t=(I-K_tA_t)\Sigma_t^-$, where $K_t$ is the Kalman gain.
Thus the Kalman filter is Bayes's theorem applied sequentially in time under Gaussian conjugacy: a probabilistic prediction step using the dynamic model, followed by a Bayesian update with the new data.

front: Present the Chapter 1 medical-imaging inverse problem: model $X=A\theta+\varepsilon$ with $\varepsilon\sim N(0,\Gamma)$. Explain why classical inversion is problematic, set up a Gaussian prior, and give the resulting Gaussian posterior mean and covariance.
back:
An unknown image $\theta\in\mathbb{R}^d$ (e.g. discretized X-ray attenuation coefficients) is observed via data $X\in\mathbb{R}^m$ through a forward operator $A:\mathbb{R}^d\to\mathbb{R}^m$ (e.g. line integrals in tomography) with noise $\varepsilon\sim N(0,\Gamma)$, i.e. $X\mid\theta\sim N(A\theta,\Gamma)$. Many such inverse problems are ill-posed: a direct inversion of $A$ is unstable or impossible (e.g. if $A$ integrates, its inverse is an unbounded differentiation operator), so small perturbations in $X$ can produce large changes in $\theta$; hence regularization is needed. A classical penalized estimator is
$$\hat\theta=\arg\min_\theta\big(\|X-A\theta\|_{\Gamma^{-1}}^2+\lambda\|\theta\|^2\big),$$
but it provides no uncertainty quantification. The Bayesian approach places a Gaussian prior $\theta\sim N(m_0,C_0)$, with log-likelihood $\log\ell(\theta,X)=-\tfrac12(A\theta-X)^\top\Gamma^{-1}(A\theta-X)$. For linear $A$, prior and likelihood Gaussian $\Rightarrow$ posterior Gaussian:
$$\theta\mid X\sim N(m,C),\quad C^{-1}=C_0^{-1}+A^\top\Gamma^{-1}A,\quad m=C\big(C_0^{-1}m_0+A^\top\Gamma^{-1}X\big).$$

points.label: Model $X\mid\theta\sim N(A\theta,\Gamma)$ and ill-posedness / need for regularization
Gaussian prior $\theta\sim N(m_0,C_0)$ and Gaussian-posterior conjugacy

front: Define a $\textbf{dominated}$ statistical model. In this context, what are the likelihood function and the log-likelihood?
back: A statistical model $(P_\theta : \theta \in \Theta)$ on a sample space $\mathcal{X}$ is called $\textbf{dominated}$ if there exists a measure $\mu$ on $\mathcal{X}$ such that every $P_\theta$ has a density with respect to $\mu$, denoted $p_\theta = dP_\theta/d\mu$. Given an observation $x$, the $\textbf{likelihood function}$ is the map $\theta \mapsto p_\theta(x)$, and whenever $p_\theta(x) > 0$ the $\textbf{log-likelihood}$ is $\ell(x,\theta) = \log p_\theta(x)$. (Typically $\mu$ is Lebesgue measure on Euclidean $\mathcal{X}$, or counting measure on a discrete $\mathcal{X}$.)

points.label: Existence of dominating measure $\mu$ with $p_\theta = dP_\theta/d\mu$

front: Define a $\textbf{Bayesian statistical model}$. State the joint density, the marginal (prior predictive) density of $x$, and the posterior distribution it gives rise to.
back:
A $\textbf{Bayesian statistical model}$ consists of a dominated model $(P_\theta : \theta \in \Theta)$ with likelihood $p_\theta(x)$, together with a $\textbf{prior distribution}$ $\Pi(\theta)$ on $\Theta$ (almost always with density $\pi$). Written as $\theta \sim \Pi(\cdot)$, $\;X|\theta \sim P_\theta(\cdot)$. It gives rise to:

â€˘  $\textbf{Joint density}$ of $(\theta,x)$: $\varphi(\theta,x) = p_\theta(x)\,\pi(\theta)$.

â€˘  $\textbf{Marginal density}$ (prior predictive) of $x$: $m(x) = \int \varphi(\theta,x)\,d\theta = \int p_\theta(x)\pi(\theta)\,d\theta$.

â€˘  $\textbf{Posterior distribution}$ via Bayes's formula: $\pi(\theta|x) = \dfrac{p_\theta(x)\pi(\theta)}{\int p_\theta(x)\pi(\theta)\,d\theta} = \dfrac{p_\theta(x)\pi(\theta)}{m(x)}$, and $\Pi(B|x) = \int_B \pi(\theta|x)\,d\theta$ for measurable $B \subseteq \Theta$.

The posterior is left undefined where $m(x)=0$ (a null set). When the data are random, the posterior is a random probability measure on $\Theta$.

front: Define a level $1-\alpha$ $\textbf{credible set}$ for a posterior $\Pi(\cdot|X)$. How does it differ from a frequentist confidence set, and what is the one general link between them?
back:
Given a posterior distribution $\Pi(\cdot|X)$, a $\textbf{level } 1-\alpha \textbf{ credible set}$ is a measurable subset $C_\alpha = C_\alpha(X) \subseteq \Theta$ such that
$$\Pi(C_\alpha(X)|X) \ge 1-\alpha.$$
(Sometimes 'credible set' refers to the set-valued mapping $X \mapsto C_\alpha(X)$.)

This is reminiscent of, but different from, a frequentist $\textbf{confidence set}$, which has $\textbf{coverage}$ $1-\alpha$: $P_\theta(\theta \in C_\alpha(X)) \ge 1-\alpha$ for all $\theta \in \Theta$. The only link in general is that a $1-\alpha$ credible set has $\textbf{average coverage}$ $1-\alpha$ under the prior:
$$\int \Pi(C_\alpha(X)|X)\,dP(X) = P(\theta \in C_\alpha) = \int P_\theta(C_\alpha(X) \ni \theta)\,d\Pi(\theta).$$

points.label: Contrast with confidence set / coverage $P_\theta(\theta\in C_\alpha)\ge 1-\alpha$

front: In the normal location model $X_1,\dots,X_n \overset{iid}{\sim} N(\theta,1)$ with prior $\theta \sim N(0,1)$, the posterior is $N\!\big(\tfrac{1}{n+1}\sum X_i, \tfrac{1}{n+1}\big)$, so the posterior mean is $\bar\theta = \tfrac{n}{n+1}\bar X$. State the CramĂ©r-Rao lower bound and show $\bar\theta$ attains it as an equality.
back:
The $\textbf{CramĂ©r-Rao lower bound}$ states that for any estimator $\hat\theta$,
$$\mathrm{Var}_\theta(\hat\theta) \ge \frac{\big(\partial_\theta \mathbb{E}_\theta[\hat\theta]\big)^2}{i(\theta)},$$
where $i(\theta)$ is the Fisher information. For the posterior mean $\bar\theta = \tfrac{n}{n+1}\bar X$ in the $N(\theta,1)$ model:

â€˘  $\mathbb{E}_\theta[\bar\theta] = \tfrac{n}{n+1}\theta$, so $\partial_\theta \mathbb{E}_\theta[\bar\theta] = \tfrac{n}{n+1}$, giving numerator $\big(\tfrac{n}{n+1}\big)^2$.

â€˘  Fisher information for $n$ i.i.d. $N(\theta,1)$ observations is $i(\theta) = n$.

â€˘  RHS $= \dfrac{(n/(n+1))^2}{n} = \dfrac{n}{(n+1)^2}$.

â€˘  Directly, $\mathrm{Var}_\theta(\bar\theta) = \big(\tfrac{n}{n+1}\big)^2 \mathrm{Var}_\theta(\bar X) = \big(\tfrac{n}{n+1}\big)^2 \cdot \tfrac{1}{n} = \dfrac{n}{(n+1)^2}$.

Both sides coincide, so $\bar\theta$ realizes the CramĂ©r-Rao lower bound as an equality.

front: Define a $\textbf{Markov kernel}$ from a measurable space $(\mathcal{X},\mathcal{A})$ into $(\mathcal{Y},\mathcal{B})$. How is a statistical model, and the marginal of $X$, expressed in this language?
back:
A $\textbf{Markov kernel}$ from $(\mathcal{X},\mathcal{A})$ into $(\mathcal{Y},\mathcal{B})$ is a map
$$Q : \mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{B} \to [0,1]$$
such that

(i)  for every $x \in \mathcal{X}$, the map $B \mapsto Q(x,B)$ is a probability measure on $(\mathcal{Y},\mathcal{B})$;

(ii)  for every $B \in \mathcal{B}$, the map $x \mapsto Q(x,B)$ is $\mathcal{A}$-measurable.

A statistical model $(P_\theta : \theta \in \Theta)$ can be seen as a Markov kernel from $(\Theta,\mathcal{B})$ into $(\mathcal{X},\mathcal{A})$: each $A \mapsto P_\theta(A)$ is a probability measure, and one assumes $\theta \mapsto P_\theta(A)$ measurable for every $A$. The marginal distribution of $X$ is then $P(A) := \int_\Theta P_\theta(A)\,d\Pi(\theta)$.

points.label: (i) $B\mapsto Q(x,B)$ a probability measure for each $x$
Model as kernel $\Theta\to\mathcal{X}$ and marginal $P(A)=\int P_\theta(A)d\Pi$

front: Give the $\textbf{measure-theoretic definition}$ of a $\textbf{posterior distribution}$ for a model $(P_\theta:\theta\in\Theta)$ (as a Markov kernel) with prior $\Pi$. Why is it only defined for $P$-almost every $x$?
back:
Given a model $(P_\theta : \theta \in \Theta)$ (as a Markov kernel) and a prior probability $\Pi$, a $\textbf{posterior distribution}$ is a Markov kernel $(x,B) \mapsto \Pi(B|x)$ from $(\mathcal{X},\mathcal{A})$ into $(\Theta,\mathcal{B})$ such that, for every $A \in \mathcal{A}$ and $B \in \mathcal{B}$,
$$\Pr(X \in A,\ \theta \in B) = \int_A \Pi(B|x)\,dP(x),$$
where $(X,\theta)$ follows the joint law of the Bayesian model and $P$ is the marginal law of $X$. The definition is via integration (rather than conditioning on $\{X=x\}$) because such events are typically null. Modifying $x \mapsto \Pi(\cdot|x)$ on a $P$-null set yields again a valid posterior, so the posterior is only defined for $P$-almost every $x \in \mathcal{X}$.

points.label: Posterior is a Markov kernel $(x,B)\mapsto\Pi(B|x)$ from $\mathcal{X}$ into $\Theta$
Only defined $P$-a.e. (modification on null set still valid)

front: State the $\textbf{existence (and essential uniqueness) of the posterior distribution}$ (Proposition 2.10). Under what assumption on $(\Theta,\mathcal{B})$ does it hold?
back:
$\textbf{Assumption:}$ $(\Theta,\mathcal{B})$ is a separable and complete metric space (a $\textbf{Polish space}$) with $\mathcal{B}$ its Borel $\sigma$-algebra.

$\textbf{Conclusion:}$ There exists a Markov kernel $(x,B) \mapsto \Pi(B|x)$ from $(\mathcal{X},\mathcal{A})$ into $(\Theta,\mathcal{B})$ satisfying the definition of the posterior (Definition 2.9). Furthermore, any other such Markov kernel $(x,B) \mapsto \Pi_1(B|x)$ satisfies $\Pi_1(B|x) = \Pi(B|x)$ for every $B \in \mathcal{B}$, for all $x$ except possibly in a set $N$ with $P(X \in N) = 0$, where $N$ can be chosen $\textbf{independently of }B$.

points.label: Polish-space assumption on $(\Theta,\mathcal{B})$ (separable complete metric, Borel)
Existence of posterior Markov kernel satisfying Def. 2.9
Essential uniqueness: agreement for all $B$ off a single $P$-null set $N$

front: State $\textbf{Bayes's formula}$ (Proposition 2.11): under a dominating $\sigma$-finite measure $\mu$ with jointly measurable densities $p_\theta(x)$, give a version of the posterior and its density with respect to the prior.
back:
$\textbf{Assumption:}$ there exists a $\sigma$-finite measure $\mu$ on the sample space $(\mathcal{X},\mathcal{A})$ and jointly measurable maps $(x,\theta) \mapsto p_\theta(x)$ such that $P_\theta(A) = \int_A p_\theta(x)\,d\mu(x)$ for every $A \in \mathcal{A}$.

$\textbf{Conclusion:}$ A version of the posterior distribution is
$$\Pi(B|x) = \frac{\int_B p_\theta(x)\,d\Pi(\theta)}{\int p_\theta(x)\,d\Pi(\theta)},$$
and its density with respect to the prior distribution is
$$\frac{d\Pi(\cdot|x)}{d\Pi}(\theta) = \frac{p_\theta(x)}{\int p_\vartheta(x)\,d\Pi(\vartheta)}.$$

front: In decision theory, give the loss function for the hypothesis testing / classification decision problem (Example 3.1).
back:
Take $\Theta = D = \{0,1\}$ (parameter space equals action/decision space). The natural loss is the $0$--$1$ loss
$$L(a,\theta) = \mathbf{1}\{a \neq \theta\}.$$
Here a decision rule $\delta : \mathcal{X} \to \{0,1\}$ is a classifier, and the risk $R(\delta,\theta)=P_\theta(\delta(X)\neq\theta)$ is a type I or type II error probability depending on $\theta$.

front: State the standard loss functions used for parametric point estimation (Example 3.2).
back:
Let $\Theta \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, action space $A = \Theta$, and decision rule $\delta(X) = \hat\theta(X)$. Two common loss functions are
$$L(a,\theta) = (a-\theta)^2 \quad\text{(squared/quadratic loss)},$$
$$L(a,\theta) = |a-\theta| \quad\text{(absolute loss)}.$$
Under squared loss the risk $R(\hat\theta,\theta)=E_\theta(\hat\theta-\theta)^2$ is the mean squared error (MSE).

front: State the conditions under which the risk function $\theta \mapsto R(\theta,\delta)$ is continuous at $\theta_0$ (Lemma 3.3, continuity of the risk).
back:
**đź“Ž Preuve laissĂ©e en exercice dans le cours** â€” secondaire : si tu manques de temps, retiens surtout l'Ă©noncĂ©.

Let $(P_\theta : \theta\in\Theta)$ be a dominated model such that for every $x\in\mathcal{X}$, the map $\theta \mapsto p_\theta(x)$ is continuous, and for every $a\in D$ the map $\theta\mapsto L(\theta,a)$ is continuous. Suppose moreover that $\sup_{\theta\in B} L(\theta,\delta(x))\,p_\theta(x)$ is integrable in $x$ for $B$ some neighborhood of $\theta_0$. Then for any decision rule $\delta:\mathcal{X}\to D$, the risk function $\theta\mapsto R(\theta,\delta)$ is continuous at $\theta_0$.

Proof idea: continuity of the likelihood and of the loss gives pointwise continuity of the integrand $L(\theta,\delta(x))p_\theta(x)$; the integrable local envelope lets one apply dominated convergence to pass the limit inside $R(\theta,\delta)=\int L(\theta,\delta(x))p_\theta(x)\,d\mu(x)$.

points.label: Conclusion: continuity of risk + DCT proof idea

front: For estimating $\theta$ in a $\mathrm{Bin}(n,\theta)$ model, $\theta\in[0,1]$, under quadratic risk, compute the risk of the MLE $\hat\theta = X/n$ and of the naive estimator $\tilde\theta = 1/2$ (Example 3.4).
back:
For the MLE $\hat\theta(X) = X/n$:
$$R(\hat\theta,\theta) = \mathrm{Var}_\theta(\hat\theta) = \frac{\theta(1-\theta)}{n}.$$
For the constant (naive) estimator $\tilde\theta = 1/2$:
$$R(\tilde\theta,\theta) = (\theta - 1/2)^2.$$
The naive estimator is preferable (smaller risk) when $\theta$ is sufficiently close to $1/2$. This illustrates that risk functions of different rules cannot be ordered uniformly in $\theta$.

front: Define the $\pi$-Bayes risk of a decision rule $\delta$ and the $\pi$-Bayes rule (Definition 3.5).
back:
Given a prior $\pi$ on $\Theta$ and model $\{P_\theta:\theta\in\Theta\}$, the $\pi$-Bayes risk of a decision rule $\delta$ is
$$R_\pi(\delta) := \int_\Theta R(\delta,\theta)\,\pi(\theta)\,d\theta = E\big[L(\delta(X),\theta)\big],$$
where in the final expectation both $\theta$ and $X$ are random, with $X|\theta \sim p_\theta(\cdot)$ and marginal density of $\theta$ equal to $\pi$. Any minimiser of $R_\pi(\delta)$ over decision rules $\delta$ is called a $\pi$-Bayes rule.

front: Under the uniform prior $\pi = \mathbf{1}_{[0,1]}$ in the Bernoulli model, compute the Bayes risk of the MLE $\hat\theta = X/n$ and of the constant rule $\delta = 1/2$ (Example 3.6).
back:
For the MLE $\hat\theta$:
$$R_\pi(\hat\theta) = \frac{1}{n}\int_0^1 \theta(1-\theta)\,d\theta = \frac{1}{6n}.$$
For the constant rule $\delta = 1/2$:
$$R_\pi(1/2) = \int_0^1 (\theta - 1/2)^2\,d\theta = \frac{1}{12}.$$
These are obtained by averaging the pointwise risks $R(\hat\theta,\theta)=\theta(1-\theta)/n$ and $R(1/2,\theta)=(\theta-1/2)^2$ over the uniform prior.

front: For squared loss, show that the posterior mean is the Bayes rule (Example 3.7, posterior mean).
back:
**Setup.** Following the decision-theoretic framework of Section 3.1, let $A = \Theta \subseteq \mathbb{R}^p$ be a **convex** action set and consider the squared (quadratic) loss
$$L(\hat\theta,\theta) = \|\hat\theta-\theta\|^2 = \sum_{i=1}^p (\hat\theta_i-\theta_i)^2 ,$$
where $\|\cdot\|$ is the Euclidean norm on $\mathbb{R}^p$, with associated inner product $\langle u,v\rangle = \sum_{i=1}^p u_i v_i$. Let $\Pi(\cdot\mid x)$ denote the posterior distribution of $\theta$ given $X=x$, and assume $E[\|\theta\|^2\mid X=x]<\infty$ (so the posterior risk is finite and the posterior mean exists). Define the **posterior mean**
$$\bar\theta = \bar\theta(x) := E[\theta\mid X=x] = \int_\Theta \theta\,\Pi(d\theta\mid x) \in \mathbb{R}^p .$$

By the discussion preceding Example 3.7, a $\pi$-Bayes rule is obtained by choosing, for each fixed $x\in\mathcal{X}$, an action $\delta=\delta(x)\in A$ that minimises the **posterior risk**
$$r_x(\delta) := E[L(\delta,\theta)\mid X=x] = E\big[\|\delta-\theta\|^2\,\big|\,X=x\big] .$$
Here $\delta$ is a fixed (deterministic) point of $A$: once $x$ is fixed, $\delta=\delta(x)$ is a constant vector, while $\theta$ is random with law $\Pi(\cdot\mid x)$. We claim the unique minimiser is $\delta=\bar\theta(x)$.

**Step 1: $\bar\theta$ is an admissible action.** Since $A=\Theta$ is convex and $\bar\theta=E[\theta\mid X=x]$ is the barycenter of the posterior distribution, which is supported on $\Theta$, convexity guarantees $\bar\theta\in\Theta = A$. (For a probability measure on a convex set, its mean lies in that set.) Thus $\bar\theta$ is a valid action, and minimising over $A$ at the value $\bar\theta$ is legitimate.

**Step 2: the bias-variance decomposition (vector form).** Fix $x$. Insert and subtract $\bar\theta$ inside the norm and expand using bilinearity of the inner product:
$$\|\delta-\theta\|^2 = \|(\delta-\bar\theta)+(\bar\theta-\theta)\|^2 = \|\delta-\bar\theta\|^2 + 2\langle \delta-\bar\theta,\ \bar\theta-\theta\rangle + \|\bar\theta-\theta\|^2 .$$
This is an exact identity in $\mathbb{R}^p$; the middle term is an **inner product** (not a scalar product), which is the correct object for $p\ge 1$. Taking the conditional expectation given $X=x$ and using its linearity,
$$r_x(\delta) = \|\delta-\bar\theta\|^2 \;+\; 2\,E\big[\langle \delta-\bar\theta,\ \bar\theta-\theta\rangle\,\big|\,X=x\big] \;+\; E\big[\|\bar\theta-\theta\|^2\,\big|\,X=x\big] ,$$
where $\|\delta-\bar\theta\|^2$ has been pulled out of the expectation because, with $x$ fixed, both $\delta=\delta(x)$ and $\bar\theta=\bar\theta(x)$ are constants.

**Step 3: the cross term vanishes.** Fix $x$. The vector $\delta-\bar\theta$ is a deterministic function of $x$, hence a **constant** given $X=x$. Therefore it factors out of the conditional expectation:
$$E\big[\langle \delta-\bar\theta,\ \bar\theta-\theta\rangle\,\big|\,X=x\big] = \big\langle \delta-\bar\theta,\ E[\bar\theta-\theta\mid X=x]\big\rangle .$$
Now, by linearity of conditional expectation and the very definition of $\bar\theta$,
$$E[\bar\theta-\theta\mid X=x] = \bar\theta - E[\theta\mid X=x] = \bar\theta - \bar\theta = 0 \in \mathbb{R}^p .$$
Hence $\langle \delta-\bar\theta,\ 0\rangle = 0$, and the entire cross term is zero. This yields the identity
$$r_x(\delta) = \|\delta-\bar\theta\|^2 \;+\; E\big[\|\bar\theta-\theta\|^2\,\big|\,X=x\big] .$$

**Step 4: minimisation.** In the last display, the second term
$$E\big[\|\bar\theta-\theta\|^2\,\big|\,X=x\big]$$
is the posterior variance (trace of the posterior covariance); it does **not** depend on $\delta$. The first term satisfies $\|\delta-\bar\theta\|^2 \ge 0$ for every $\delta\in A$, with equality **if and only if** $\delta=\bar\theta$ (the Euclidean norm vanishes only at the zero vector). Therefore
$$r_x(\delta) \ge E\big[\|\bar\theta-\theta\|^2\,\big|\,X=x\big] = r_x(\bar\theta),$$
with equality iff $\delta=\bar\theta$. Hence $\delta=\bar\theta(x)$ is the **unique** minimiser of the posterior risk at each $x$.

**Conclusion.** Setting $\delta_\Pi(x) := \bar\theta(x) = E[\theta\mid X=x]$ minimises the posterior risk for every $x\in\mathcal{X}$, and $\delta_\Pi(x)\in A$ by Step 1. By the characterisation of $\pi$-Bayes rules preceding this example, $\delta_\Pi$ is the (essentially unique, i.e. $P$-almost surely unique under the prior-predictive law $P$) $\pi$-Bayes rule for quadratic loss. That is, **the posterior mean is the Bayes rule under squared loss**. $\qquad\blacksquare$

front: For absolute loss, which point estimator is the Bayes rule? (Example 3.8, posterior median.)
back:
**đź“Ž Preuve laissĂ©e en exercice dans le cours** â€” secondaire : si tu manques de temps, retiens surtout l'Ă©noncĂ©.

**Setting (Example 3.8).** Take $A=\Theta\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ and the absolute loss $L(\hat\theta,\theta)=|\hat\theta-\theta|$. By the reduction in Definition 3.5 / Example 3.6, a $\pi$-Bayes rule is obtained by choosing, for each fixed $x\in\mathcal X$, the action $\delta(x)$ minimising the **posterior risk**
$$g(t)\;:=\;E\big[\,|t-\theta|\;\big|\;X=x\big]\;=\;\int_\Theta |t-\theta|\,\Pi(\theta\mid x)\,d\theta,\qquad t\in\mathbb{R}.$$
Here $t$ is the free candidate action (we write $t$, not $\hat\theta$, to keep it cleanly separate from the random $\theta$). Let
$$F(t):=\Pi(\theta\le t\mid x),\qquad F(t^-):=\lim_{s\uparrow t}F(s)=\Pi(\theta<t\mid x)$$
be the posterior CDF of $\theta$ given $X=x$ and its left limit; $F$ is non-decreasing and **right-continuous**, so $F(t^+)=F(t)$, while in general $F(t^-)\le F(t)$, with equality iff there is no posterior atom at $t$. The posterior atom is
$$\Pi(\theta=t\mid x)=F(t)-F(t^-)\ge 0.$$
A **posterior median** is any $m\in\mathbb{R}$ with
$$F(m^-)\le \tfrac12\le F(m).$$

**Claim.** $g$ is minimised precisely at the posterior medians. Hence the posterior median is the Bayes rule under absolute loss.

We assume $g(t)<\infty$ for some $t$; then $g(t)<\infty$ for all $t$ since $\big|\,|t-\theta|-|t'-\theta|\,\big|\le|t-t'|$.

**Step 1: $g$ is finite, convex and continuous.**
For each fixed $\theta$ the map $t\mapsto|t-\theta|$ is convex. An average of convex functions is convex, so $g$ is convex on $\mathbb{R}$, hence continuous. Therefore $g$ attains its infimum on any compact set, and $g$ possesses finite left and right derivatives $g'_-(t)\le g'_+(t)$ at every $t$, with $t$ a minimiser **iff** $0\in[g'_-(t),g'_+(t)]$ (the subdifferential of a convex function).

**Step 2: One-sided derivatives of $g$.**
We compute the right and left derivatives separately by dominated convergence, being careful about the value of the difference quotient at the point $\theta=t$ (the only place where the two computations differ, and exactly the case posterior atoms make relevant).

**Right derivative.** Fix $t$ and $h>0$. Pointwise in $\theta$,
$$\frac{|t+h-\theta|-|t-\theta|}{h}=\begin{cases}+1,&\theta\le t,\\[4pt]\dfrac{|t+h-\theta|-|t-\theta|}{h}\in[-1,1],&t<\theta<t+h,\\[4pt]-1,&\theta\ge t+h.\end{cases}$$
At the point $\theta=t$ this quotient equals $\dfrac{|h|-0}{h}=+1$ for **every** $h>0$, so $\theta=t$ falls in the first ($+1$) branch. As $h\downarrow0$ the middle region $\{t<\theta<t+h\}$ shrinks to $\varnothing$, and the quotient converges pointwise to
$$q_+(\theta):=\mathbf 1\{\theta\le t\}-\mathbf 1\{\theta>t\}\qquad(\text{the value }+1\text{ being attained at }\theta=t).$$
Each quotient is bounded in absolute value by $1$, and the constant $1$ is $\Pi(\cdot\mid x)$-integrable. By the **dominated convergence theorem** we may interchange limit and integral:
$$g'_+(t)=\lim_{h\downarrow0}\int_\Theta\frac{|t+h-\theta|-|t-\theta|}{h}\,\Pi(\theta\mid x)\,d\theta=\int_\Theta q_+(\theta)\,\Pi(\theta\mid x)\,d\theta.$$
Hence, since the $+1$ branch includes $\{\theta=t\}$,
$$g'_+(t)=\Pi(\theta\le t\mid x)-\Pi(\theta>t\mid x)=F(t)-\big(1-F(t)\big)=2F(t)-1.$$

**Left derivative.** Now take $h<0$, i.e. $h\uparrow0$; equivalently put $k=-h>0$ and form the left difference quotient $\dfrac{|t-k-\theta|-|t-\theta|}{-k}=\dfrac{|t-\theta|-|t-k-\theta|}{k}$. Pointwise in $\theta$ it equals $+1$ for $\theta\le t-k$, lies in $[-1,1]$ for $t-k<\theta<t$, and equals $-1$ for $\theta\ge t$. At the point $\theta=t$ it equals $\dfrac{0-|{-k}|}{k}=-1$, so now $\theta=t$ falls in the $-1$ branch. As $k\downarrow0$ it converges pointwise to
$$q_-(\theta):=\mathbf 1\{\theta<t\}-\mathbf 1\{\theta\ge t\}\qquad(\text{the value }-1\text{ being attained at }\theta=t),$$
again dominated by $1$. By dominated convergence,
$$g'_-(t)=\Pi(\theta<t\mid x)-\Pi(\theta\ge t\mid x)=F(t^-)-\big(1-F(t^-)\big)=2F(t^-)-1.$$

So
$$\boxed{\,g'_+(t)=2F(t)-1,\qquad g'_-(t)=2F(t^-)-1.\,}$$
The only difference between the two computations is the membership of the atom $\{\theta=t\}$: in the right quotient it sits in the $+1$ branch, in the left quotient in the $-1$ branch. Consequently the jump
$$g'_+(t)-g'_-(t)=2\big(F(t)-F(t^-)\big)=2\,\Pi(\theta=t\mid x)\ge0$$
is non-negative, consistent with convexity, and is exactly $0$ when there is no atom at $t$ (so $g$ is differentiable there).

**Step 3: Characterisation of minimisers.**
By Step 1, $t$ minimises $g$ iff $g'_-(t)\le0\le g'_+(t)$, i.e.
$$2F(t^-)-1\le0\le 2F(t)-1\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad F(t^-)\le\tfrac12\le F(t).$$
This is exactly the defining property of a posterior median. Thus the set of minimisers of $g$ equals the set of posterior medians (a single point, or an interval where $F\equiv\tfrac12$); convexity guarantees this set is non-empty and is precisely the optimum.

**Step 4: Direct verification (independent of differentiation).**
For completeness we confirm $g(t)\ge g(m)$ for every candidate $t$ and every median $m$, without invoking derivatives (this also covers atoms / flat regions cleanly). Fix a median $m$ and take $t>m$. For each $\theta$,
$$|t-\theta|-|m-\theta|=\begin{cases}\;t-m,&\theta\le m,\\[4pt]\;(t-\theta)-(\theta-m)=t+m-2\theta\ \ge\ -(t-m),&m<\theta<t,\\[4pt]\;-(t-m),&\theta\ge t.\end{cases}$$
In all three regions $|t-\theta|-|m-\theta|\ge (t-m)\big(\mathbf 1\{\theta\le m\}-\mathbf 1\{\theta>m\}\big)$. Integrating against $\Pi(\cdot\mid x)$,
$$g(t)-g(m)=\int_\Theta\big(|t-\theta|-|m-\theta|\big)\,\Pi(\theta\mid x)\,d\theta\ \ge\ (t-m)\big[\Pi(\theta\le m\mid x)-\Pi(\theta>m\mid x)\big]=(t-m)\big(2F(m)-1\big)\ \ge\ 0,$$
since $t-m>0$ and $F(m)\ge\tfrac12$. The case $t<m$ is symmetric: there $|t-\theta|-|m-\theta|\ge(m-t)\big(\mathbf 1\{\theta\ge m\}-\mathbf 1\{\theta<m\}\big)$, giving
$$g(t)-g(m)\ge(m-t)\big[\Pi(\theta\ge m\mid x)-\Pi(\theta<m\mid x)\big]=(m-t)\big(1-2F(m^-)\big)\ge0,$$
using $F(m^-)\le\tfrac12$. Hence $g(t)\ge g(m)$ for all $t$, so every posterior median minimises $g$.

**Conclusion.**
For each fixed $x$, the posterior risk $g$ is minimised exactly at the posterior medians $m=m(x)$. By Definition 3.5, setting $\delta_\Pi(x)=m(x)$ for every $x$ yields a $\pi$-Bayes rule, and (when the median is unique) it is **the** $\pi$-Bayes rule. Therefore, under absolute loss $L(\hat\theta,\theta)=|\hat\theta-\theta|$, the Bayes point estimator is the **posterior median**. $\qquad\blacksquare$

points.label: Proof idea via differentiating posterior risk (DCT)

front: For binary classification with $0$--$1$ loss, which rule is the Bayes rule? (Example 3.9, posterior mode.)
back:
For binary classification with $0$--$1$ loss $L(a,\theta)=\mathbf{1}\{a\neq\theta\}$, $\theta\in\{0,1\}$, the posterior risk is minimised by the posterior mode. Fixing $x$, the posterior risk is
$$E[L(\delta,\theta)\,|\,X=x] = P(\delta\neq\theta\,|\,X=x) = \begin{cases}P(\theta=1|x), & \delta=0,\\ P(\theta=0|x), & \delta=1.\end{cases}$$
Hence it is minimised by
$$\delta(x) = \arg\max_{k\in\{0,1\}} P(\theta=k|x),$$
i.e. the class with largest posterior probability â€” the Bayes classifier.

front: State and prove that an unbiased estimator that is also $\pi$-Bayes for quadratic risk must equal $\theta$ almost surely (Proposition 3.10).
back:
Let $\Theta\subseteq\mathbb{R}$. Suppose $\hat\theta=\hat\theta(X)$ is unbiased, $E_\theta(\hat\theta)=\theta$, and is also $\pi$-Bayes for some prior $\pi$ under quadratic risk. Then $P(\hat\theta=\theta)=1$, where $P$ is the joint law under the Bayesian model.

Proof: It suffices to show $E(\hat\theta-\theta)^2 = E(\hat\theta^2)-2E(\theta\hat\theta)+E(\theta^2)=0$. By unbiasedness,
$$E(\theta\hat\theta)=E[\theta\,E[\hat\theta\,|\,\theta]]=E(\theta^2).$$
Since $\hat\theta$ is $\pi$-Bayes for quadratic risk, $\hat\theta=E(\theta|X)$ a.s., so
$$E(\theta\hat\theta)=E\{\theta\,E(\theta|X)\}=E\{E(\theta\,E(\theta|X)\,|\,X)\}=E(\hat\theta^2).$$
Combining, $E(\hat\theta-\theta)^2 = E(\hat\theta^2)-2E(\theta^2)+E(\theta^2)=E(\hat\theta^2)-E(\theta^2)=0$. Hence $\hat\theta=\theta$ a.s. Consequence: Bayes estimators are inherently biased.

front: For $X\sim\mathrm{Bin}(n,\theta)$, compute the worst-case (maximum) risk of the MLE $\hat\theta=X/n$ and of the constant rule $1/2$ (Example 3.13).
back:
For the MLE $\hat\theta = X/n$:
$$\sup_{\theta\in[0,1]} R(\hat\theta,\theta) = \sup_{\theta\in[0,1]} \frac{\theta(1-\theta)}{n} = \frac{1}{4n},$$
attained at $\theta=1/2$. For the constant rule $\delta=1/2$:
$$\sup_{\theta\in[0,1]} R(1/2,\theta) = \sup_{\theta}(\theta-1/2)^2 = \frac{1}{4},$$
attained at $\theta\in\{0,1\}$. Thus the MLE has far smaller worst-case risk.

front: Define the worst-case risk of a decision rule, the minimax risk of a model, and a minimax decision rule (Definition 3.14).
back:
The worst-case risk of a decision rule $\delta$ is
$$\bar R(\delta) = \sup_{\theta\in\Theta} R(\delta,\theta).$$
The minimax risk of the model $(P_\theta:\theta\in\Theta)$ with respect to a loss $L$ is the best achievable worst-case risk
$$\inf_{\delta}\sup_{\theta\in\Theta} R(\delta,\theta).$$
A decision rule $\delta$ whose maximal risk $\sup_{\theta\in\Theta} R(\delta,\theta)$ attains the minimax risk is said to be minimax.

front: State and prove the theorem giving a sufficient condition for a $\pi$-Bayes rule to be minimax (Theorem 3.15).
back:
Let $\pi$ be a prior on $\Theta$ and suppose $\delta_\pi$ is a decision rule with
$$R_\pi(\delta_\pi) = \sup_{\theta\in\Theta} R(\delta_\pi,\theta).$$
(This holds in particular if $\delta_\pi$ has constant risk in $\theta$.) If $\delta_\pi$ is a (unique) $\pi$-Bayes rule, then it is a (unique) minimax rule.

Proof: For any decision rule $\delta$,
$$\sup_{\theta} R(\delta,\theta) \ge \int_\Theta R(\delta,\theta)\pi(\theta)\,d\theta = R_\pi(\delta) \ge R_\pi(\delta_\pi) = \sup_\theta R(\delta_\pi,\theta),$$
using that the sup dominates the average, and that $\delta_\pi$ is $\pi$-Bayes. Hence $\inf_\delta \sup_\theta R(\delta,\theta)=\sup_\theta R(\delta_\pi,\theta)$, so $\delta_\pi$ is minimax. If $\delta_\pi$ is the unique $\pi$-Bayes rule, the second inequality is strict for $\delta\neq\delta_\pi$, so no other rule attains the minimax risk; $\delta_\pi$ is the unique minimax rule.

front: Define a least favourable prior (Definition 3.16) and explain why a prior whose Bayes risk equals the minimax risk is least favourable.
back:
A prior $\pi$ is least favourable if, given a $\pi$-Bayes estimator $\delta_\pi$, for any other prior $\lambda$ with $\lambda$-Bayes estimator $\delta_\lambda$ we have
$$R_\lambda(\delta_\lambda) \le R_\pi(\delta_\pi).$$

Why: if $\pi$ is a prior whose Bayes risk equals the minimax risk, then for any other prior $\lambda$,
$$R_\lambda(\delta_\lambda) \le R_\lambda(\delta_\pi) \le \sup_\theta R(\delta_\pi,\theta) = R_\pi(\delta_\pi),$$
where the first inequality holds because $\delta_\lambda$ minimises $\lambda$-Bayes risk, the second because sup dominates average, and the equality by assumption. Hence $\pi$ is least favourable.

points.label: Justification chain for $\pi$ achieving minimax risk

front: In the Binomial model, explain how to construct a minimax estimator using a $\mathrm{Beta}(a,b)$ prior (Example 3.17).
back:
One can show the MLE $\hat\theta=X/n$ is not minimax. Instead, take a $\mathrm{Beta}(a,b)$ prior $\pi_{a,b}$ on $\theta\in[0,1]$ and, writing $\bar\theta_{a,b}$ for the posterior mean, solve for $(a,b)$ the equation
$$R(\bar\theta_{a,b},\theta) = \text{const} \quad\text{for all } \theta\in[0,1].$$
This yields a unique Bayes rule with constant risk, which (by Theorem 3.15) is therefore the unique minimax rule. The corresponding $\pi_{a,b}$ is least favourable.

points.label: Unique constant-risk Bayes rule $\Rightarrow$ unique minimax

front: Define when a decision rule is inadmissible and when it is admissible (Definition 3.18).
back:
A decision rule $\delta$ is inadmissible if there exists another decision rule $\delta'$ that dominates $\delta$, meaning
$$R(\delta',\theta) \le R(\delta,\theta) \quad\text{for all }\theta\in\Theta,$$
$$R(\delta',\theta) < R(\delta,\theta) \quad\text{for some }\theta\in\Theta.$$
Otherwise $\delta$ is admissible. This is the natural partial order among risk functions. Note: a constant estimator can be admissible, and a minimax rule need not be admissible.

front: State and prove the relations between unique Bayes rules, admissibility, minimaxity and constant risk (Proposition 3.19).
back:
**đź“Ž Note** â€” dans le cours, **seule la partie (iii)** est laissĂ©e en exercice ; les parties (i)â€“(ii) sont dĂ©montrĂ©es en cours.

**Setup and notation (as in Â§3.2â€“3.3).** For a decision rule $\delta$ and parameter $\theta\in\Theta$, write $R(\delta,\theta)$ for the (frequentist) risk. For a prior with density $\pi$ on $\Theta$, the **$\pi$-Bayes risk** is
$$R_\pi(\delta)=\int_\Theta R(\delta,\theta)\,\pi(\theta)\,d\theta,$$
and $\delta_\pi$ denotes a **$\pi$-Bayes rule**, i.e. one minimizing $R_\pi$. Per **Definition 3.18**, $\delta'$ **dominates** $\delta$ iff $R(\delta',\theta)\le R(\delta,\theta)$ for all $\theta\in\Theta$ and $R(\delta',\theta_0)<R(\delta,\theta_0)$ for some $\theta_0$; $\delta$ is **admissible** if no such $\delta'$ exists. Per **Definition 3.14**, $\delta$ is **minimax** if $\sup_\theta R(\delta,\theta)=\inf_{\tilde\delta}\sup_\theta R(\tilde\delta,\theta)$.

**Statement (Proposition 3.19).**
(i) If the $\pi$-Bayes rule for a prior $\pi$ is unique, it is admissible.
(ii) If a decision rule $\delta$ is admissible and has constant risk, it is minimax.
(iii) Suppose $\Theta\subseteq\mathbb{R}^p$. If $\theta\mapsto R(\delta,\theta)$ is continuous for every decision rule $\delta$ and the prior has density $\pi>0$ everywhere on $\Theta$, then the $\pi$-Bayes estimator $\delta_\pi$ is admissible.

**Proof.**

**(i)** Let $\delta_\pi$ be the unique $\pi$-Bayes rule. Suppose, for contradiction, that $\delta_\pi$ is inadmissible: by Definition 3.18 there is $\delta$ with $R(\delta,\theta)\le R(\delta_\pi,\theta)$ for all $\theta$ (and strict somewhere). Integrating this pointwise inequality against $\pi\ge 0$,
$$R_\pi(\delta)=\int_\Theta R(\delta,\theta)\,\pi(\theta)\,d\theta\le\int_\Theta R(\delta_\pi,\theta)\,\pi(\theta)\,d\theta=R_\pi(\delta_\pi).$$
Since $\delta_\pi$ minimizes $R_\pi$ we also have $R_\pi(\delta_\pi)\le R_\pi(\delta)$, so $R_\pi(\delta)=R_\pi(\delta_\pi)$; hence $\delta$ is itself $\pi$-Bayes. By **uniqueness** $\delta=\delta_\pi$, contradicting that $\delta$ dominates $\delta_\pi$ strictly at some point. Therefore $\delta_\pi$ is admissible. $\square$

**(ii)** Let $\delta$ be admissible with constant risk $R(\delta,\theta)\equiv c$, so $\sup_\theta R(\delta,\theta)=c$. Suppose $\delta$ is not minimax. Then there exists $\delta'$ with
$$\sup_\theta R(\delta',\theta)<\sup_\theta R(\delta,\theta)=c.$$
For every $\theta$ this gives $R(\delta',\theta)\le\sup_{\theta'}R(\delta',\theta')<c=R(\delta,\theta)$, i.e. $R(\delta',\theta)<R(\delta,\theta)$ for **all** $\theta$. Thus $\delta'$ dominates $\delta$ (Definition 3.18, with strict inequality everywhere), contradicting admissibility of $\delta$. Hence $\delta$ is minimax. $\square$

**(iii)** Assume $\Theta\subseteq\mathbb{R}^p$, that $\theta\mapsto R(\delta,\theta)$ is continuous for every $\delta$, and that $\pi>0$ on all of $\Theta$. Suppose, for contradiction, that the $\pi$-Bayes rule $\delta_\pi$ is **inadmissible**. By Definition 3.18 there is a rule $\delta'$ that dominates it:
$$R(\delta',\theta)\le R(\delta_\pi,\theta)\quad\text{for all }\theta\in\Theta,\qquad R(\delta',\theta_0)<R(\delta_\pi,\theta_0)\ \text{ for some }\theta_0\in\Theta.$$
Define the **risk gap**
$$g(\theta):=R(\delta_\pi,\theta)-R(\delta',\theta).$$
By the continuity hypothesis applied to both $\delta_\pi$ and $\delta'$, $g$ is continuous on $\Theta$. By domination, $g(\theta)\ge 0$ everywhere and $g(\theta_0)>0$. Set $\varepsilon:=g(\theta_0)/2>0$.

**Positive gap on a neighborhood.** Since $g$ is continuous at $\theta_0$ and $g(\theta_0)=2\varepsilon>\varepsilon$, there is a radius $r>0$ such that on the open ball
$$U:=\{\theta\in\Theta:\|\theta-\theta_0\|<r\}$$
we have $g(\theta)\ge\varepsilon>0$ for all $\theta\in U$. (Concretely: $\{\theta:g(\theta)>\varepsilon\}$ is open by continuity and contains $\theta_0$, so it contains such a ball intersected with $\Theta$.)

**The gap has strictly positive $\pi$-mass.** Because $\theta_0\in\Theta$ and $\Theta\subseteq\mathbb{R}^p$, the open neighborhood $U$ has positive Lebesgue measure, $\mathrm{Leb}(U)>0$. Since $\pi>0$ everywhere on $\Theta$ (in particular on $U$), it follows that
$$\int_U \pi(\theta)\,d\theta>0,$$
as the integral of a strictly positive function over a set of positive Lebesgue measure.

**Contradiction via the Bayes risk.** Using $g\ge 0$ on all of $\Theta$ and $g\ge\varepsilon$ on $U$,
$$R_\pi(\delta_\pi)-R_\pi(\delta')=\int_\Theta g(\theta)\,\pi(\theta)\,d\theta\ge\int_U g(\theta)\,\pi(\theta)\,d\theta\ge\varepsilon\int_U\pi(\theta)\,d\theta>0.$$
Hence $R_\pi(\delta')<R_\pi(\delta_\pi)$, contradicting that $\delta_\pi$ minimizes the $\pi$-Bayes risk. Therefore no dominating $\delta'$ exists, i.e. $\delta_\pi$ is admissible. $\square$

**Remark.** Part (iii) needs no uniqueness assumption: continuity of the risk together with a strictly positive prior density already forces any $\pi$-Bayes rule to be admissible, since any strict improvement, being continuous, persists on a neighborhood of positive $\pi$-mass and would strictly lower the Bayes risk.

points.label: Proof of (ii) by contradiction using constant risk

front: State the CramĂ©r--Rao lower bound for a (possibly biased) estimator (Lemma 3.20).
back:
Suppose $\Theta\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ is open and the dominated statistical model has sufficiently smooth densities $p_\theta(\cdot)$. For any estimator $\tilde\theta$, denote its bias function by $b(\theta):=E_\theta(\tilde\theta)-\theta$. Then for every $\theta\in\Theta$,
$$\mathrm{Var}_\theta(\tilde\theta) \ge \frac{(b'(\theta)+1)^2}{I(\theta)},$$
where $I(\theta)=\mathrm{Var}(\partial_\theta \log p_\theta(X))$ is the Fisher information.

front: Prove that for $X_1,\dots,X_n \stackrel{iid}{\sim} N(\theta,1)$, $\theta\in\mathbb{R}$, the MLE $\hat\theta=\bar X$ is admissible and minimax (Theorem 3.21).
back:
Statement: For the model $X_1,\dots,X_n\stackrel{iid}{\sim}N(\theta,1)$, $\theta\in\mathbb{R}$, the MLE $\hat\theta=\bar X$ is (i) admissible and (ii) minimax.

Proof. The MLE has constant risk $R(\hat\theta,\theta)=E_\theta(\bar X-\theta)^2=1/n$, so by Proposition 3.19(ii) it suffices to show admissibility. For any estimator $\tilde\theta$ with bias $b(\theta)$, the variance--bias decomposition and the CramĂ©r--Rao bound (Lemma 3.20, $I(\theta)=n$) give
$$R(\tilde\theta,\theta) = b(\theta)^2 + \mathrm{Var}_\theta(\tilde\theta) \ge b(\theta)^2 + \tfrac1n(1+b'(\theta))^2.$$
If $R(\tilde\theta,\theta)\le R(\hat\theta,\theta)=1/n$, then $b(\theta)^2+\tfrac1n(1+b'(\theta))^2\le \tfrac1n$, forcing $(1+b'(\theta))^2\le1$, i.e. $-2\le b'(\theta)\le 0$. So $b$ is nonincreasing and bounded. By the mean value theorem there are sequences $\theta_k^\pm\to\pm\infty$ with $b'(\theta_k^\pm)\to0$, whence $b(\theta_k^\pm)\to0$. Since $b$ is nonincreasing, $\lim_{\theta\to-\infty}b(\theta)\ge b(\theta)\ge \lim_{\theta\to+\infty}b(\theta)$; both limits are $0$, so $b\equiv0$. Then $R(\tilde\theta,\theta)=1/n=R(\hat\theta,\theta)$, so $\hat\theta$ is admissible (and being constant-risk admissible, it is minimax).

front: State and sketch the proof that the MLE $X$ is inadmissible for the quadratic risk in the $N(\theta,I_d)$ model when $p\ge3$ (Theorem 3.22, James--Stein phenomenon).
back:
**Statement (Theorem 3.22).** Consider the normal model $X\mid\theta\sim N(\theta,I_p)$ in dimension $p\ge 3$, observed once, with quadratic risk $R(\hat\theta,\theta)=E_\theta\|\hat\theta-\theta\|^2$. Then the MLE $\hat\theta_{\mathrm{MLE}}(X)=X$ is **inadmissible** (Definition 3.18): the James-Stein estimator
$$\hat\theta_{\mathrm{JS}}(X)=\Big(1-\frac{p-2}{\|X\|^2}\Big)X$$
satisfies $R(\hat\theta_{\mathrm{JS}},\theta)<R(\hat\theta_{\mathrm{MLE}},\theta)$ for **every** $\theta\in\mathbb{R}^p$.

Throughout, $\|\cdot\|$ is the Euclidean norm on $\mathbb{R}^p$, $I_p$ the identity matrix, and we write $X=\theta+Z$ with $Z\sim N(0,I_p)$ under $P_\theta$. We use the dimension symbol $p$ uniformly (so $I_p$, $I_d$ with $d=p$ are the same; the course's $d$ in the divergence formula is $p$).

**Step 0: Baseline risk of the MLE.** Since $X-\theta=Z$,
$$R(\hat\theta_{\mathrm{MLE}},\theta)=E_\theta\|X-\theta\|^2=E\|Z\|^2=\sum_{i=1}^p E[Z_i^2]=\operatorname{trace}(I_p)=p .$$
This $p$ is the benchmark every comparison must beat.

**Step 1: Risk decomposition for shrinkage estimators.** Consider $\hat\theta=X-g(X)$ for a vector field $g:\mathbb{R}^p\to\mathbb{R}^p$. Using $X-\theta=Z$,
$$R(\hat\theta,\theta)=E_\theta\|Z-g(X)\|^2=E_\theta\|Z\|^2-2E_\theta[Z^Tg(X)]+E_\theta\|g(X)\|^2=p-\underbrace{2E_\theta[Z^Tg(X)]}_{=:I}+\underbrace{E_\theta\|g(X)\|^2}_{=:II}.$$
All terms are finite for the $g$ chosen below (Step 3), so the splitting is legitimate.

**Step 2: Stein's lemma, multivariate form, and its hypotheses.** The course's one-dimensional Stein's lemma (Lemma 3.23) states: if $Z_1\sim N(0,1)$ and $h:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ is absolutely continuous with $E|h'(Z_1)|<\infty$ and $\lim_{|x|\to\infty}h(x)\varphi(x)=0$ (where $\varphi$ is the standard normal density), then $E[Z_1\,h(Z_1)]=E[h'(Z_1)]$.

I claim the following multivariate consequence. Let $g=(g_1,\dots,g_p):\mathbb{R}^p\to\mathbb{R}^p$ be such that, for each $i$, $x_i\mapsto g_i(\theta+z)$ is absolutely continuous in the $i$-th coordinate for a.e. fixing of the others, with $E_\theta|\partial_{i}g_i(X)|<\infty$ and $E_\theta|g_i(X)|<\infty$, and the boundary terms vanish (verified below). Then, writing $\operatorname{div}g=\sum_{i=1}^p\partial_i g_i$,
$$E_\theta[Z^Tg(X)]=E_\theta[\operatorname{div}g(X)].$$
**Proof of the multivariate identity.** Fix $i$. Conditioning on the coordinates $Z_j$, $j\ne i$, and applying Lemma 3.23 in the single variable $z_i$ to $h(z_i):=g_i\big(\theta+(z_1,\dots,z_i,\dots,z_p)\big)$ (whose derivative is $\partial_i g_i(\theta+z)$), the one-dimensional identity gives
$$E\big[Z_i\,g_i(\theta+Z)\,\big|\,Z_j,\ j\ne i\big]=E\big[\partial_i g_i(\theta+Z)\,\big|\,Z_j,\ j\ne i\big].$$
Taking expectations and summing over $i$ yields $E_\theta[Z^Tg(X)]=\sum_i E_\theta[Z_ig_i(X)]=\sum_i E_\theta[\partial_i g_i(X)]=E_\theta[\operatorname{div}g(X)]$, as claimed.

**Step 3: The shrinkage field and its divergence.** Take
$$g(x)=\frac{\alpha\,x}{\|x\|^2},\qquad x\neq 0,\ \alpha\in\mathbb{R}.$$
This is singular only at the origin, a Lebesgue-null set, and is smooth on $\mathbb{R}^p\setminus\{0\}$. We compute its divergence there. With $g_i(x)=\alpha x_i/\|x\|^2$,
$$\partial_i g_i(x)=\alpha\,\partial_i\!\Big(\frac{x_i}{\|x\|^2}\Big)=\alpha\Big(\frac{1}{\|x\|^2}-\frac{2x_i^2}{\|x\|^4}\Big),$$
so summing over $i$ (using $\sum_i x_i^2=\|x\|^2$),
$$\operatorname{div}g(x)=\sum_{i=1}^p\partial_i g_i(x)=\alpha\Big(\frac{p}{\|x\|^2}-\frac{2\|x\|^2}{\|x\|^4}\Big)=\frac{\alpha(p-2)}{\|x\|^2}.$$

**Step 4: Finiteness, the role of $p\ge 3$, and validity of Stein's lemma for this singular $g$.** The whole argument requires $E_\theta\big[\|X\|^{-2}\big]<\infty$; this is exactly where $p\ge 3$ enters.

Fix $\theta$. Split according to $\|X\|$. On $\{\|X\|\ge 1\}$ we have $\|X\|^{-2}\le 1$. On the ball $B=\{x:\|x\|<1\}$, the $N(\theta,I_p)$ density is bounded by $c_p:=(2\pi)^{-p/2}$, so passing to polar coordinates ($dx=r^{p-1}\,dr\,d\sigma$ on the sphere of surface area $\omega_{p-1}$),
$$E_\theta\Big[\frac{\mathbf 1_{B}}{\|X\|^2}\Big]\le c_p\int_{B}\frac{dx}{\|x\|^2}=c_p\,\omega_{p-1}\int_0^1\frac{r^{p-1}}{r^2}\,dr=c_p\,\omega_{p-1}\int_0^1 r^{p-3}\,dr.$$
The integral $\int_0^1 r^{p-3}\,dr$ converges **iff** $p-3>-1$, i.e. $p\ge 3$ (it equals $1/(p-2)$). Hence
$$E_\theta\Big[\frac1{\|X\|^2}\Big]<\infty\quad\text{for }p\ge 3,$$
while for $p\le 2$ the same computation gives $\int_0^1 r^{p-3}\,dr=\infty$, and since the $N(\theta,I_p)$ density is also bounded **below** by a positive constant on $B$, $E_\theta[\|X\|^{-2}]=\infty$. This is precisely why the dimension hypothesis $p\ge 3$ is needed and where it is used: the dominating estimator's risk correction is finite only then.

This finiteness also legitimizes applying the multivariate Stein identity to the singular $g$. Indeed:
- $E_\theta|g_i(X)|\le \alpha\,E_\theta[\|X\|^{-1}]\le\alpha\big(E_\theta[\|X\|^{-2}]\big)^{1/2}<\infty$ (Cauchy-Schwarz, then Step 4), and $E_\theta|\partial_i g_i(X)|\le 3|\alpha|\,E_\theta[\|X\|^{-2}]<\infty$, so all integrals appearing in Step 2 are finite.
- The function $z_i\mapsto g_i(\theta+z)$ is absolutely continuous on every interval avoiding the (at most two) points where $\|\theta+z\|=0$; the single possible singularity is integrable because $\int |\partial_i g_i|\,d(\text{Gaussian})<\infty$, so $g_i(\theta+\cdot)$ is the integral of its a.e.-derivative and the one-dimensional integration-by-parts of Lemma 3.23 holds on the complement of a null set. The Gaussian boundary term $g_i(\theta+z)\varphi(z_i)\to 0$ as $|z_i|\to\infty$ since $|g_i|\le \alpha/\|x\|\le \alpha/|x_i|\to 0$ faster than $\varphi$ decays is multiplied. Hence the hypotheses of Lemma 3.23 hold coordinatewise (a.e. conditioning), and Step 2 applies to this $g$.

**Step 5: Evaluating the two terms.** By Steps 2 and 3,
$$I=2E_\theta[Z^Tg(X)]=2E_\theta[\operatorname{div}g(X)]=2\alpha(p-2)\,E_\theta\Big[\frac1{\|X\|^2}\Big],$$
and directly, since $\|g(x)\|^2=\alpha^2\|x\|^2/\|x\|^4=\alpha^2/\|x\|^2$,
$$II=E_\theta\|g(X)\|^2=\alpha^2\,E_\theta\Big[\frac1{\|X\|^2}\Big].$$
Both are finite by Step 4.

**Step 6: Optimizing $\alpha$ and conclusion.** Substituting into Step 1,
$$R(\hat\theta,\theta)=p-I+II=p-\big(2\alpha(p-2)-\alpha^2\big)E_\theta\Big[\frac1{\|X\|^2}\Big]=p+\big(\alpha^2-2\alpha(p-2)\big)E_\theta\Big[\frac1{\|X\|^2}\Big].$$
The scalar $\alpha\mapsto \alpha^2-2\alpha(p-2)$ is a parabola minimized at $\alpha=p-2$, where it equals $-(p-2)^2$. Choosing $\alpha=p-2$ gives $g(x)=(p-2)x/\|x\|^2$, i.e. $\hat\theta=X-g(X)=\big(1-(p-2)/\|X\|^2\big)X=\hat\theta_{\mathrm{JS}}(X)$, and
$$R(\hat\theta_{\mathrm{JS}},\theta)=p-(p-2)^2\,E_\theta\Big[\frac1{\|X\|^2}\Big].$$
For $p\ge 3$ we have $(p-2)^2>0$ and, by Step 4, $0<E_\theta[\|X\|^{-2}]<\infty$ (the expectation is strictly positive since $\|X\|^{-2}>0$ a.s.). Therefore the subtracted term is strictly positive and finite, so
$$R(\hat\theta_{\mathrm{JS}},\theta)<p=R(\hat\theta_{\mathrm{MLE}},\theta)\qquad\text{for every }\theta\in\mathbb{R}^p.$$
Thus $\hat\theta_{\mathrm{JS}}$ dominates the MLE with strict inequality at every $\theta$ (in particular at some $\theta$, as Definition 3.18 requires). Hence the MLE $X$ is inadmissible for the quadratic risk when $p\ge 3$. $\blacksquare$

**Remark.** The hypothesis $p\ge 3$ is not an artifact: for $p\le 2$ the correction term $(p-2)^2E_\theta[\|X\|^{-2}]$ is either zero ($p=2$) or built on an infinite expectation ($p\le 2$, where $E_\theta[\|X\|^{-2}]=\infty$), and indeed the MLE is admissible in dimensions $1$ and $2$. The phenomenon â€” that shrinking a Gaussian mean estimate strictly improves total risk â€” is genuinely a high-dimensional effect.

points.label: Choice $g=\alpha x/\|x\|^2$, Stein's lemma to get $I$

front: State Stein's lemma (Gaussian integration by parts) (Lemma 3.23).
back:
**â„ąď¸Ź Non dĂ©montrĂ© dans le cours** (lemme admis) â€” concentre-toi sur l'Ă©noncĂ©.

Let $Z\sim N(0,1)$ and let $g:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ be differentiable with
$$E|g'(Z)| < \infty \quad\text{and}\quad \lim_{|x|\to\infty} g(x)\phi(x) = 0,$$
where $\phi(x)=(2\pi)^{-1/2}e^{-x^2/2}$ is the standard normal density. Then
$$E[Z g(Z)] = E[g'(Z)].$$

Proof idea: integrate by parts using $\phi'(x)=-x\phi(x)$; the boundary term vanishes by the decay assumption $g(x)\phi(x)\to0$.

front: Why does shrinking $X$ towards the origin improve the risk in high dimensions, even though it induces a large error near the origin? (Remark 3.24, interpretation of James--Stein.)
back:
The James--Stein correction induces a comparatively large error when $X$ is close to the origin, yet still improves the overall risk because in high dimensions the noise vector $Z\sim N(0,I_d)$ is typically of size $\sqrt{p}$. Indeed,
$$E_\theta\|X\|^2 = \|\theta\|^2 + E_\theta\|Z\|^2 = \|\theta\|^2 + p.$$
Thus when $\|X\|^2$ is of order $p$, a significant component of $X$ comes from the noise rather than the signal, so shrinking towards the origin is reasonable and reduces the mean squared error.

points.label: Noise is of size $\sqrt{p}$ in high dimension
Interpretation: large noise component justifies shrinkage

front: State and prove the complete class theorem in the finite setting (Theorem 3.25).
back:
**Setting.** $\Theta=\{\theta_1,\dots,\theta_m\}$, sample space $\mathcal{X}$ and decision space $D$ are all finite; the loss $L(\theta,a)\in\mathbb{R}$. A **randomized rule** $\delta$ assigns to each $x\in\mathcal{X}$ a probability distribution $\delta(\cdot\mid x)$ on $D$:
$$\delta(a\mid x)\ge 0,\qquad \sum_{a\in D}\delta(a\mid x)=1\quad\forall x\in\mathcal{X}.$$
Its **risk** and, for a prior $\pi$ on $\Theta$, its **Bayes risk** are
$$R(\theta,\delta)=\sum_{x\in\mathcal{X}}P_\theta(x)\sum_{a\in D}\delta(a\mid x)L(\theta,a),\qquad R_\pi(\delta)=\sum_{\theta\in\Theta}\pi(\theta)\,R(\theta,\delta).$$
$\delta$ is **inadmissible** (Def. 3.18) if some $\delta'$ dominates it: $R(\theta,\delta')\le R(\theta,\delta)$ for all $\theta$, with strict inequality for some $\theta$; otherwise $\delta$ is **admissible**.

**Theorem 3.25.** Every admissible rule is Bayes for some prior $\pi$ on $\Theta$. Moreover, if $\delta$ is Bayes for a full-support prior ($\pi(\theta)>0$ for all $\theta$), then $\delta$ is admissible.

The proof uses the **risk set**
$$\mathcal{R}=\big\{(R(\theta_1,\delta),\dots,R(\theta_m,\delta)):\delta\text{ a randomized rule}\big\}\subset\mathbb{R}^m.$$

**Lemma 3.26 (the risk set is compact and convex).**
The set of randomized rules is exactly the product of probability simplices
$$S=\prod_{x\in\mathcal{X}}\Delta(D)\subset\mathbb{R}^{|D||\mathcal{X}|},\qquad \Delta(D)=\Big\{v\in\mathbb{R}^{|D|}:v_a\ge 0,\ \textstyle\sum_a v_a=1\Big\}.$$
Each $\Delta(D)$ is closed (intersection of the closed half-spaces $\{v_a\ge 0\}$ with the closed hyperplane $\{\sum_a v_a=1\}$) and bounded ($0\le v_a\le 1$), hence compact by Heine-Borel; it is convex, since a convex combination of distributions on $D$ is again a distribution. A finite product of compact convex sets is compact and convex, so $S$ is compact and convex. The risk map
$$T(\delta)=\big(R(\theta_1,\delta),\dots,R(\theta_m,\delta)\big)$$
is **linear** in $\delta=(\delta(a\mid x))_{a,x}$: for each $i$, $R(\theta_i,\delta)=\sum_{x}\sum_{a}P_{\theta_i}(x)L(\theta_i,a)\,\delta(a\mid x)$ is a fixed linear functional of the entries $\delta(a\mid x)$. A linear map on a finite-dimensional space is continuous. Therefore $\mathcal{R}=T(S)$ is the continuous image of a compact set, hence **compact**, and the linear image of a convex set, hence **convex**. $\;\square$

**Lemma 3.27 (geometric separation).**
Let $C\subset\mathbb{R}^m$ be nonempty, compact, convex, and let $z\in C$ be such that there is no $y\in C$ with $y\le z$ and $y\ne z$ (componentwise). Then there exists $\pi\in[0,\infty)^m\setminus\{0\}$ with $\pi\cdot z\le \pi\cdot y$ for all $y\in C$.

*Proof.* Put $K=C+\mathbb{R}^m_+=\{y+u:y\in C,\ u\ge 0\}$, where $\mathbb{R}^m_+=\{u:u_i\ge 0\}$. **$K$ is convex:** if $y+u,\ y'+u'\in K$ and $t\in[0,1]$, then $t(y+u)+(1-t)(y'+u')=\big(ty+(1-t)y'\big)+\big(tu+(1-t)u'\big)$, with the first bracket in $C$ (convexity of $C$) and the second in $\mathbb{R}^m_+$. **$z\notin\operatorname{int}(K)$:** suppose otherwise; then for small $\varepsilon>0$ the point $z-\varepsilon e_i\in K$ for each $i$, where $e_i$ is the $i$-th unit vector. Write $z-\varepsilon e_i=y+u$ with $y\in C$, $u\ge 0$. Then $y=z-\varepsilon e_i-u\le z$ and $y_i\le z_i-\varepsilon<z_i$, so $y\ne z$, contradicting the hypothesis on $z$. Hence $z\in\partial K$.

Since $K$ is convex and $z\in\partial K$, the **supporting hyperplane theorem** (Thm 3.29 below; $K$ is convex, and its closure is closed convex with $z$ on its boundary) gives $\pi\in\mathbb{R}^m\setminus\{0\}$ with
$$\pi\cdot z\le \pi\cdot w\qquad\text{for all }w\in K.$$
Take $w=y+t e_i$ with $y\in C$ fixed, $t>0$: then $\pi\cdot z\le \pi\cdot y+t\,\pi_i$. Letting $t\to\infty$ forces $\pi_i\ge 0$; doing this for each $i$ gives $\pi\in[0,\infty)^m\setminus\{0\}$. Taking $w=y$ ($u=0\in\mathbb{R}^m_+$) yields $\pi\cdot z\le \pi\cdot y$ for all $y\in C$. $\;\square$

**Theorem 3.28 (separation).** Let $C\subset\mathbb{R}^n$ be nonempty, closed, convex, and $y\notin C$. Then there is $\pi\in\mathbb{R}^n\setminus\{0\}$ with $\pi\cdot x\le \pi\cdot y$ for all $x\in C$.

*Proof.* Let $d=\inf_{x\in C}|y-x|$. Pick $x_k\in C$ with $|y-x_k|\to d$; the $x_k$ lie in the compact ball $\overline{B}(y,d+1)$, so a subsequence converges to some $z$, and $z\in C$ since $C$ is closed. By continuity $|y-z|=d$, and $d>0$ because $y\notin C$. For $x\in C$ and $t\in[0,1]$, convexity gives $z+t(x-z)\in C$, so $\varphi(t)=|y-z-t(x-z)|^2$ is minimized at $t=0$, whence $\varphi'(0)=-2(y-z)\cdot(x-z)\ge 0$, i.e. $(y-z)\cdot(x-z)\le 0$. Thus $(y-z)\cdot x\le (y-z)\cdot z\le (y-z)\cdot y$ (the last since $(y-z)\cdot(y-z)=d^2>0$). Setting $\pi=y-z\ne 0$ gives $\pi\cdot x\le \pi\cdot y$ for all $x\in C$. $\;\square$

**Theorem 3.29 (supporting hyperplane).** Let $C\subset\mathbb{R}^n$ be nonempty, closed, convex, and $z\in\partial C$. Then there is $\pi\in\mathbb{R}^n\setminus\{0\}$ with $\pi\cdot x\le \pi\cdot z$ for all $x\in C$.

*Proof.* Since $z\in\partial C$, choose $y_k\notin C$ with $y_k\to z$. By Thm 3.28 there are $\pi_k\ne 0$ with $\pi_k\cdot x\le \pi_k\cdot y_k$ for all $x\in C$; normalize $|\pi_k|=1$. By compactness of the unit sphere a subsequence has $\pi_k\to\pi$ with $|\pi|=1$, so $\pi\ne 0$. Passing to the limit in $\pi_k\cdot x\le \pi_k\cdot y_k$ gives $\pi\cdot x\le \pi\cdot z$ for all $x\in C$. $\;\square$

**Proof of Theorem 3.25.**

**Admissible $\Rightarrow$ Bayes.** Let $\delta$ be admissible and $z=(R(\theta_1,\delta),\dots,R(\theta_m,\delta))\in\mathcal{R}$. Admissibility means no $\delta'$ dominates $\delta$; in risk-set terms, there is no $y\in\mathcal{R}$ with $y\le z$ and $y\ne z$ (such a $y$ would be the risk vector of a dominating rule). By Lemma 3.26, $C=\mathcal{R}$ is nonempty, compact, convex, so Lemma 3.27 yields $\pi\in[0,\infty)^m\setminus\{0\}$ with
$$\pi\cdot z\le \pi\cdot y\qquad\text{for all }y\in\mathcal{R}.$$
Since $\pi\ne 0$ and $\pi_i\ge 0$, $\sum_j\pi_j>0$; normalize $\tilde\pi_i=\pi_i/\sum_j\pi_j$, a genuine prior on $\Theta$. For every randomized rule $\delta'$, applying the inequality at $y=(R(\theta_1,\delta'),\dots,R(\theta_m,\delta'))\in\mathcal{R}$ and dividing by $\sum_j\pi_j>0$,
$$R_{\tilde\pi}(\delta)=\sum_i\tilde\pi_i R(\theta_i,\delta)\le \sum_i\tilde\pi_i R(\theta_i,\delta')=R_{\tilde\pi}(\delta').$$
Hence $\delta$ minimizes the Bayes risk for $\tilde\pi$, i.e. $\delta$ is $\tilde\pi$-Bayes.

**Full-support Bayes $\Rightarrow$ admissible.** Suppose $\delta$ is Bayes for $\pi$ with $\pi(\theta)>0$ for all $\theta$, and suppose for contradiction some $\delta'$ dominates $\delta$: $R(\theta,\delta')\le R(\theta,\delta)$ for all $\theta$, strict for some $\theta_0$. Since every weight $\pi(\theta)>0$,
$$R_\pi(\delta')=\sum_\theta\pi(\theta)R(\theta,\delta')<\sum_\theta\pi(\theta)R(\theta,\delta)=R_\pi(\delta),$$
the strict inequality coming from the term $\theta_0$ (whose positive weight multiplies a strict decrease) while all other terms are $\le$. This contradicts the Bayes optimality of $\delta$. Hence no such $\delta'$ exists and $\delta$ is admissible. $\;\blacksquare$

points.label: Apply separating lemma 3.27 to get $\pi\ge0$, normalize to prior

front: State and prove that the risk set $\mathcal{R}$ is compact and convex (Lemma 3.26).
back:
In the finite complete-class setting, the risk set
$$\mathcal{R}=\{(R(\theta_1,\delta),\dots,R(\theta_m,\delta)):\delta\text{ a randomized rule}\}\subset\mathbb{R}^m_+$$
is compact and convex.

Proof. The space of randomized rules is the finite product of probability simplices $\prod_{x\in\mathcal{X}}\Delta(D)\subseteq\mathbb{R}^{|D||\mathcal{X}|}$, which is compact and convex (finite product of compact convex simplices). The map $\delta\mapsto(R(\theta_1,\delta),\dots,R(\theta_m,\delta))$ is linear (each $R(\theta_i,\delta)=\sum_x P_{\theta_i}(x)\sum_a\delta(a|x)L(\theta_i,a)$ is linear in $\delta$), hence continuous. The continuous image of a compact set is compact, and the linear image of a convex set is convex. Therefore $\mathcal{R}$ is compact and convex.

points.label: Domain = product of simplices is compact convex
Image of compact convex under linear map is compact convex

front: State and prove the geometric lemma: a point of $C$ not strictly dominated admits a nonnegative supporting normal $\pi$ (Lemma 3.27).
back:
**Statement (Lemma 3.27).** Let $C\subset\mathbb{R}^m$ be nonempty, compact, and convex, and let $z\in C$. Suppose there is no $y\in C$ with $y\le z$ and $y\neq z$ (componentwise inequality; i.e. $z$ is **not strictly dominated** in $C$). Then there exists $\pi\in[0,\infty)^m$, $\pi\neq 0$, such that
$$\pi\cdot z\le\pi\cdot y\qquad\text{for all }y\in C.$$

Throughout, $\mathbb{R}^m_+=\{u\in\mathbb{R}^m:u_i\ge 0\ \forall i\}$ and $\mathbf{1}=(1,\dots,1)$; $e_i$ is the $i$-th standard basis vector; $\mathrm{int}$ and $\partial$ denote interior and boundary. We write $a\le b$ for componentwise inequality and $a<b$ for $a_i<b_i$ for every $i$.

**Construction of the set $K$.** Define the Minkowski sum
$$K=C+\mathbb{R}^m_+=\{y+u:y\in C,\ u\in\mathbb{R}^m_+\}.$$
Geometrically $K$ collects all points of $\mathbb{R}^m$ that are dominated from below by some point of $C$ (i.e. $w\in K$ iff $w\ge y$ for some $y\in C$).

**Step 1: $K$ is convex.** Both $C$ and $\mathbb{R}^m_+$ are convex. We show the Minkowski sum of two convex sets is convex. Let $w_1=y_1+u_1$ and $w_2=y_2+u_2$ with $y_1,y_2\in C$ and $u_1,u_2\in\mathbb{R}^m_+$, and let $\lambda\in[0,1]$. Then
$$\lambda w_1+(1-\lambda)w_2=\big(\lambda y_1+(1-\lambda)y_2\big)+\big(\lambda u_1+(1-\lambda)u_2\big).$$
By convexity of $C$ the first bracket lies in $C$; since $\mathbb{R}^m_+$ is convex (indeed a cone closed under nonnegative combinations) the second bracket lies in $\mathbb{R}^m_+$. Hence $\lambda w_1+(1-\lambda)w_2\in K$, so $K$ is convex.

**Step 2: $K$ is closed.** Let $(w_k)\subset K$ with $w_k\to w$. Write $w_k=y_k+u_k$, $y_k\in C$, $u_k\in\mathbb{R}^m_+$. Since $C$ is compact, after passing to a subsequence $y_k\to y\in C$. Then $u_k=w_k-y_k\to w-y$, and $\mathbb{R}^m_+$ is closed, so $u:=w-y\in\mathbb{R}^m_+$. Thus $w=y+u\in K$, and $K$ is closed. (This is exactly the regularity the course's supporting hyperplane theorem requires.)

**Step 3: $z\in K$ and $K$ has nonempty interior.** Taking $u=0$ gives $z=z+0\in C+\mathbb{R}^m_+=K$, so $z\in K$. Moreover the open positive orthant shifted to $z$ lies in $K$: for any $u$ with $u>0$ we have $z+u=z+u\in K$ with $z\in C$, and the map $u\mapsto z+u$ sends the open set $\{u:u>0\}$ into $K$; hence $z+\mathbb{R}^m_{++}\subset K$ where $\mathbb{R}^m_{++}=\{u:u>0\}$ is open and nonempty. Therefore $\mathrm{int}(K)\neq\varnothing$.

**Step 4 (crux): $z\notin\mathrm{int}(K)$.** Suppose, for contradiction, that $z\in\mathrm{int}(K)$. Then there is $\varepsilon>0$ with the ball $B(z,\varepsilon)\subset K$. The point $z-\varepsilon'\mathbf{1}$ with $0<\varepsilon'<\varepsilon/\sqrt{m}$ satisfies $\lvert (z-\varepsilon'\mathbf{1})-z\rvert=\varepsilon'\sqrt{m}<\varepsilon$, hence $z-\varepsilon'\mathbf{1}\in K$. By definition of $K$ there exist $y\in C$ and $u\in\mathbb{R}^m_+$ with
$$z-\varepsilon'\mathbf{1}=y+u,\qquad\text{i.e.}\qquad y=z-\varepsilon'\mathbf{1}-u.$$
Since $u\ge 0$ and $\varepsilon'>0$, componentwise $y_i=z_i-\varepsilon'-u_i\le z_i-\varepsilon'<z_i$ for every $i$, so $y<z$; in particular $y\le z$ and $y\neq z$. Thus $y\in C$ strictly dominates $z$, contradicting the hypothesis. Therefore $z\notin\mathrm{int}(K)$.

**Step 5: $z\in\partial K$, so a supporting hyperplane exists.** By Step 3, $z\in K$; by Step 4, $z\notin\mathrm{int}(K)$. A point of a set that belongs to the set but not to its interior is a boundary point, so $z\in\partial K$. Now $K$ is nonempty, closed, and convex (Steps 1-2), $z\in\partial K$, and $\mathrm{int}(K)\neq\varnothing$ (Step 3). The **supporting hyperplane theorem** (Theorem 3.29) applies and yields a vector $\pi_0\in\mathbb{R}^m\setminus\{0\}$ with
$$\pi_0\cdot w\le\pi_0\cdot z\qquad\text{for all }w\in K.$$

**Step 6: sign convention.** Set $\pi:=-\pi_0$. Then $\pi\neq 0$ and, negating the inequality above,
$$\pi\cdot z\le\pi\cdot w\qquad\text{for all }w\in K.\tag{$\ast$}$$
We chose the sign $\pi=-\pi_0$ precisely so the supporting inequality opens toward $K$ in the form $\pi\cdot z\le\pi\cdot w$; the opposite choice would give the reverse inequality and break the nonnegativity argument below. (Equivalently: $z$ minimizes $w\mapsto\pi\cdot w$ over $K$.)

**Step 7: $\pi\ge 0$.** Fix any index $i$ and any $y\in C$. Since $y+te_i\in K$ for all $t>0$ (as $te_i\in\mathbb{R}^m_+$), $(\ast)$ gives
$$\pi\cdot z\le\pi\cdot(y+te_i)=\pi\cdot y+t\,\pi_i\qquad\text{for all }t>0.$$
If $\pi_i<0$, the right-hand side $\to-\infty$ as $t\to\infty$, contradicting the fixed finite lower bound $\pi\cdot z$. Hence $\pi_i\ge 0$ for every $i$, i.e. $\pi\in[0,\infty)^m$. Combined with $\pi\neq 0$, we get $\pi\in[0,\infty)^m\setminus\{0\}$.

**Step 8: conclusion.** For any $y\in C$ we have $y=y+0\in K$ (take $u=0$), so $(\ast)$ specialized to $w=y$ gives
$$\pi\cdot z\le\pi\cdot y\qquad\text{for all }y\in C.$$
This is the asserted inequality, with $\pi\in[0,\infty)^m\setminus\{0\}$ a nonnegative supporting normal at $z$. $\qquad\blacksquare$

**Remark (why nonnegativity matters here).** The orthant direction $\mathbb{R}^m_+$ built into $K$ is exactly what forces $\pi\ge 0$ in Step 7; without enlarging $C$ to $K=C+\mathbb{R}^m_+$, a bare separation of $z$ from $C$ would only give a nonzero $\pi$ of arbitrary sign. In the complete-class theorem (Theorem 3.25) this lemma is applied with $C=\mathcal{R}$ the risk set and $z=(R(\theta_1,\delta),\dots,R(\theta_m,\delta))$, and the resulting $\pi\ge 0$ normalizes to a prior $\tilde\pi_i=\pi_i/\sum_j\pi_j$ for which $\delta$ is Bayes.

front: State and prove the separation theorem for a point outside a closed convex set (Theorem 3.28).
back:
**Statement (Theorem 3.28, Separation theorem).** Let $C\subset\mathbb{R}^n$ be a nonempty closed convex set and let $y\notin C$. Then there exists $\pi\in\mathbb{R}^n\setminus\{0\}$ such that
$$\pi\cdot x \le \pi\cdot y \qquad\text{for all }x\in C.$$
Here $a\cdot b$ denotes the Euclidean inner product on $\mathbb{R}^n$ and $|a|=\sqrt{a\cdot a}$ the Euclidean norm.

**Proof.**

**Step 1: existence of the metric projection $z$ of $y$ onto $C$.**
Set
$$d:=\inf_{x\in C}|y-x|.$$
Since $C\neq\varnothing$, $d$ is a finite nonnegative number. Choose a minimizing sequence $(x_k)\subset C$ with $|y-x_k|\to d$. For all $k$ large enough $|y-x_k|\le d+1$, so the sequence $(x_k)$ eventually lies in the closed ball $B(y,d+1)=\{x:|y-x|\le d+1\}$, which is compact in $\mathbb{R}^n$ (closed and bounded, Heine-Borel). Hence $(x_k)$ has a convergent subsequence $x_{k_j}\to z$; since $C$ is closed and $x_{k_j}\in C$, the limit satisfies $z\in C$. By continuity of the norm,
$$|y-z|=\lim_{j\to\infty}|y-x_{k_j}|=d.$$
Thus $z\in C$ attains the distance: $|y-z|=d=\inf_{x\in C}|y-x|$.

**Step 2: the variational (first-order) inequality.**
Fix any $x\in C$. By convexity of $C$, the segment from $z$ to $x$ stays in $C$:
$$z+t(x-z)\in C\qquad\text{for all }t\in[0,1].$$
Since $z$ minimizes the distance from $y$ to $C$, the scalar function
$$\varphi(t):=\bigl|\,y-\bigl(z+t(x-z)\bigr)\bigr|^2,\qquad t\in[0,1],$$
satisfies $\varphi(t)\ge d^2=\varphi(0)$ for all $t\in[0,1]$, i.e. $\varphi$ attains its minimum on $[0,1]$ at the left endpoint $t=0$. Therefore its right derivative there is nonnegative: $\varphi'(0)\ge 0$.

We compute $\varphi'(0)$ explicitly. Writing $y-\bigl(z+t(x-z)\bigr)=(y-z)-t(x-z)$ and expanding the squared norm,
$$\varphi(t)=\bigl|(y-z)-t(x-z)\bigr|^2=|y-z|^2-2t\,(y-z)\cdot(x-z)+t^2\,|x-z|^2.$$
This is a quadratic polynomial in $t$, so
$$\varphi'(t)=-2\,(y-z)\cdot(x-z)+2t\,|x-z|^2,\qquad\text{hence}\qquad \varphi'(0)=-2\,(y-z)\cdot(x-z).$$
The condition $\varphi'(0)\ge 0$ thus reads $-2\,(y-z)\cdot(x-z)\ge 0$, i.e.
$$(y-z)\cdot(x-z)\le 0\qquad\text{for all }x\in C.$$
Expanding $(x-z)=x-z$ gives the equivalent form
$$(y-z)\cdot x\le (y-z)\cdot z\qquad\text{for all }x\in C. \tag{$\ast$}$$

**Step 3: the choice of $\pi$ and why $\pi\neq 0$.**
Set $\pi:=y-z$. Since $z\in C$ but $y\notin C$, we have $z\neq y$, hence
$$\pi=y-z\neq 0,$$
so $\pi\in\mathbb{R}^n\setminus\{0\}$ as required.

**Step 4: bridging $\pi\cdot z$ and $\pi\cdot y$ to reach the stated conclusion.**
Inequality $(\ast)$ gives $\pi\cdot x\le\pi\cdot z$ for all $x\in C$. It remains to replace $\pi\cdot z$ by $\pi\cdot y$. Compute the gap:
$$\pi\cdot y-\pi\cdot z=\pi\cdot(y-z)=(y-z)\cdot(y-z)=|y-z|^2\ge 0,$$
so $\pi\cdot z\le\pi\cdot y$. Chaining this with $(\ast)$,
$$\pi\cdot x\;\le\;\pi\cdot z\;\le\;\pi\cdot y\qquad\text{for all }x\in C,$$
which is exactly
$$\pi\cdot x\le\pi\cdot y\qquad\text{for all }x\in C.$$
This establishes the theorem with $\pi=y-z\neq 0$. $\qquad\blacksquare$

**Remark.** In fact $|y-z|^2=d^2>0$ since $y\notin C$ forces $d>0$, so the separation is strict at $y$: $\pi\cdot y-\sup_{x\in C}\pi\cdot x\ge d^2>0$.

points.label: Existence of nearest point $z$ (projection) via compactness

front: State and prove the supporting hyperplane theorem for a boundary point of a closed convex set (Theorem 3.29).
back:
Statement: Let $C\subset\mathbb{R}^n$ be a nonempty closed convex set and $z\in\partial C$. Then there exists $\pi\in\mathbb{R}^n\setminus\{0\}$ with
$$\pi\cdot x \le \pi\cdot z \quad\text{for all }x\in C,$$
so $H=\{x:\pi\cdot x=\pi\cdot z\}$ is a supporting hyperplane to $C$ at $z$.

Proof. Since $z\in\partial C$, choose a sequence $(y_k)\subset\mathbb{R}^n\setminus C$ with $y_k\to z$. For each $k$, the separation theorem (Thm 3.28) gives $\pi_k\neq0$ with $\pi_k\cdot x\le\pi_k\cdot y_k$ for all $x\in C$. Normalize $|\pi_k|=1$. By compactness of the unit sphere, a subsequence satisfies $\pi_k\to\pi$ with $|\pi|=1$, so $\pi\neq0$. Passing to the limit in $\pi_k\cdot x\le\pi_k\cdot y_k$ yields $\pi\cdot x\le\pi\cdot z$ for all $x\in C$. Thus $H$ supports $C$ at $z$.

points.label: Approximate $z$ by exterior points $y_k\to z$, apply separation thm
Normalize $|\pi_k|=1$, compactness of sphere gives limit $\pi\neq0$

front: Define (weak and strong) posterior consistency at a point $\theta_0$ for a sequence of posterior distributions $\Pi_n(\cdot\mid X^{(n)})$, where $d$ is a metric on $\Theta$. What does it mean intuitively?
back:
Let $d$ be a metric on $\Theta$. The posterior distributions $\Pi_n(\cdot\mid X^{(n)})$ are said to be **(weakly) consistent** at $\theta_0\in\Theta$ if for every $\varepsilon>0$,
$$\Pi_n\big(\{\theta: d(\theta,\theta_0)>\varepsilon\}\mid X^{(n)}\big)\longrightarrow 0$$
in $P^{(n)}_{\theta_0}$-probability as $n\to\infty$. They are **strongly consistent** at $\theta_0$ if this convergence holds almost surely.

Intuitively, the posterior mass contracts to within arbitrarily small distance $\varepsilon$ of the true parameter $\theta_0$: the posteriors 'converge to a Dirac measure at $\theta_0$'.

points.label: Weak consistency: $\Pi_n(d(\theta,\theta_0)>\varepsilon\mid X^{(n)})\to0$ in $P_{\theta_0}^{(n)}$-probability for every $\varepsilon>0$
Strong consistency: same convergence holds almost surely
Interpretation: posterior contracts to a Dirac at $\theta_0$

front: State Proposition 4.2: how does posterior consistency at $\theta_0$ yield a consistent point estimator? Define the estimator $\hat\theta_n$ (center of smallest ball containing posterior mass $\geq m$) and the conclusion.
back:
**đź“Ž Preuve laissĂ©e en exercice dans le cours** â€” secondaire : si tu manques de temps, retiens surtout l'Ă©noncĂ©.

**Setup.** Parameter space $(\Theta, d)$ a metric space; observations $X^{(n)}$ with true law $P_{\theta_0}$; posteriors $\Pi_n(\cdot\mid X^{(n)})$. Write $B_\delta(\theta)=\{\vartheta\in\Theta: d(\vartheta,\theta)<\delta\}$.

**Consistency at $\theta_0$ (equivalent characterizations).** The posteriors are (strongly) consistent at $\theta_0$ w.r.t. $d$ iff for every $\varepsilon>0$
$$\Pi_n\big(d(\theta,\theta_0)>\varepsilon \,\big|\, X^{(n)}\big)\to 0 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \Pi_n\big(B_\varepsilon(\theta_0)\,\big|\,X^{(n)}\big)\to 1,$$
the convergence being in $P_{\theta_0}$-probability (weak) resp. $P_{\theta_0}$-a.s. (strong).

**Construction of the point estimator.** Fix any $m\in(0,1)$. For $\theta\in\Theta$ define the radius of the smallest ball centered at $\theta$ carrying posterior mass $\geq m$:
$$R_n(\theta)=\inf\{\delta>0: \Pi_n(B_\delta(\theta)\mid X^{(n)})\geq m\},$$
and set
$$\hat\theta_n\in\operatorname*{arg\,min}_{\theta\in\Theta} R_n(\theta),$$
i.e. $\hat\theta_n$ is the center of a smallest ball carrying mass $\geq m$.

**Proposition 4.2.** If $\Pi_n(\cdot\mid X^{(n)})$ is (strongly) consistent at $\theta_0$ w.r.t. $d$, then $d(\hat\theta_n,\theta_0)\to 0$ in $P_{\theta_0}$-probability (resp. $P_{\theta_0}$-a.s.).

**Existence / measurability of the argmin.** $\theta\mapsto R_n(\theta)$ need not attain its infimum, and a measurable exact minimizer need not exist in general. Two standard remedies, either of which suffices:
- **Near-minimizer.** Pick a measurable $\hat\theta_n$ with $R_n(\hat\theta_n)\leq \inf_\theta R_n(\theta)+1/n$; the extra $1/n\to 0$ does not affect the conclusion. (Measurable selection of an $\eta$-near-minimizer of a measurable function of $(\theta, X^{(n)})$ exists, e.g. by the Kuratowskiâ€“Ryll-Nardzewski / measurable-projection theorem, or trivially when $\Theta$ is separable by minimizing over a countable dense set.)
- **Exact minimizer.** If $\Theta$ is, say, a separable complete metric space and $\delta\mapsto\Pi_n(B_\delta(\theta))$ together with lower semicontinuity of $\theta\mapsto R_n(\theta)$ on a $\sigma$-compact $\Theta$ guarantees attainment, take a measurable exact selection $\hat\theta_n$.

Below I argue with an exact minimizer; for a near-minimizer replace $\inf_\theta R_n=R_n(\hat\theta_n)$ by $R_n(\hat\theta_n)\le\inf_\theta R_n+1/n$ throughout, which only adds $1/n$ to every bound.

**Proof.** Fix $\varepsilon>0$. All "$\Pi_n$" below mean $\Pi_n(\cdot\mid X^{(n)})$, and all radii/quantities depend on $X^{(n)}$.

**(a) Control of the optimal radius: $R_n(\hat\theta_n)\leq \varepsilon$ eventually.**
By consistency, $\Pi_n(B_\varepsilon(\theta_0))\to 1$ (in prob. resp. a.s.). Hence, on the event
$$A_n:=\big\{\Pi_n(B_\varepsilon(\theta_0))> 1-m\big\}\cap\big\{\Pi_n(B_\varepsilon(\theta_0))\geq m\big\},$$
which has $P_{\theta_0}(A_n)\to 1$ (resp. holds for all large $n$ a.s.) since $1-m<1$ and $m<1$, the ball $B_\varepsilon(\theta_0)$ carries posterior mass $\geq m$. By definition of the infimum $R_n$,
$$R_n(\theta_0)\leq \varepsilon \quad\text{on } A_n,$$
and therefore, since $\hat\theta_n$ minimizes $R_n$,
$$R_n(\hat\theta_n)=\min_{\theta} R_n(\theta)\leq R_n(\theta_0)\leq \varepsilon \quad\text{on } A_n.$$

**(b) The two balls must intersect.**
By definition of $R_n(\hat\theta_n)$ as an infimum of radii achieving mass $\geq m$, for every $\eta>0$ the ball $B_{R_n(\hat\theta_n)+\eta}(\hat\theta_n)$ has posterior mass $\geq m$; letting $\eta\downarrow 0$ along a sequence and using that $B_{R_n(\hat\theta_n)+\eta}(\hat\theta_n)\downarrow \overline{B}_{R_n(\hat\theta_n)}(\hat\theta_n)$, the closed ball $\overline{B}_{R_n(\hat\theta_n)}(\hat\theta_n)$ has posterior mass $\geq m$ (by continuity of the measure from above). On $A_n$ the open ball $B_\varepsilon(\theta_0)$ has mass $>1-m$. If these two sets were disjoint, a probability measure would assign them total mass
$$\Pi_n\big(\overline{B}_{R_n(\hat\theta_n)}(\hat\theta_n)\big)+\Pi_n\big(B_\varepsilon(\theta_0)\big)\;>\; m+(1-m)=1,$$
which is impossible for the probability measure $\Pi_n(\cdot\mid X^{(n)})$. Hence on $A_n$
$$\overline{B}_{R_n(\hat\theta_n)}(\hat\theta_n)\cap B_\varepsilon(\theta_0)\neq\varnothing.$$
Pick a common point $\theta^{\ast}$ (a measurable selection again exists, but only its existence is needed). Then $d(\hat\theta_n,\theta^{\ast})\leq R_n(\hat\theta_n)$ and $d(\theta^{\ast},\theta_0)<\varepsilon$.

**(c) Triangle inequality and conclusion.**
On $A_n$, combining (a) and (b),
